When man's worst sin is, he does too much good!
Who, then, dares to be half so kind again?
For bounty, that makes gods, does still mar men.
My dearest lord, bless'd, to be most accursed,
Rich, only to be wretched, thy great fortunes
Are made thy chief afflictions. Alas, kind lord!
He's flung in rage from this ingrateful seat
Of monstrous friends, nor has he with him to
Supply his life, or that which can command it.
I'll follow and inquire him out:
I'll ever serve his mind with my best will;
Whilst I have gold, I'll be his steward still.
Exit
Enter TIMON, from the cave

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
O blessed breeding sun, draw from the earth
Rotten humidity; below thy sister's orb
Infect the air! Twinn'd brothers of one womb,
Whose procreation, residence, and birth,
Scarce is dividant, touch them with several fortunes;
The greater scorns the lesser: not nature,
To whom all sores lay siege, can bear great fortune,
But by contempt of nature.
Raise me this beggar, and deny 't that lord;
The senator shall bear contempt hereditary,
The beggar native honour.
It is the pasture lards the rother's sides,
The want that makes him lean. Who dares, who dares,
In purity of manhood stand upright,
And say 'This man's a flatterer?' if one be,
So are they all; for every grise of fortune
Is smooth'd by that below: the learned pate
Ducks to the golden fool: all is oblique;
There's nothing level in our cursed natures,
But direct villany. Therefore, be abhorr'd
All feasts, societies, and throngs of men!
His semblable, yea, himself, Timon disdains:
Destruction fang mankind! Earth, yield me roots!

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Who seeks for better of thee, sauce his palate
With thy most operant poison! What is here?
Gold? yellow, glittering, precious gold? No, gods,
I am no idle votarist: roots, you clear heavens!
Thus much of this will make black white, foul fair,
Wrong right, base noble, old young, coward valiant.
Ha, you gods! why this? what this, you gods? Why, this
Will lug your priests and servants from your sides,
Pluck stout men's pillows from below their heads:
This yellow slave
Will knit and break religions, bless the accursed,
Make the hoar leprosy adored, place thieves
And give them title, knee and approbation
With senators on the bench: this is it
That makes the wappen'd widow wed again;
She, whom the spital-house and ulcerous sores
Would cast the gorge at, this embalms and spices
To the April day again. Come, damned earth,
Thou common whore of mankind, that put'st odds
Among the route of nations, I will make thee
Do thy right nature.
March afar off

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Ha! a drum ? Thou'rt quick,
But yet I'll bury thee: thou'lt go, strong thief,
When gouty keepers of thee cannot stand.
Nay, stay thou out for earnest.
Enter ALCIBIADES, with drum and fife, in warlike manner; PHRYNIA and TIMANDRA

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text: What art thou there? speak.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
A beast, as thou art. The canker gnaw thy heart,
For showing me again the eyes of man!

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text:
What is thy name? Is man so hateful to thee,
That art thyself a man?

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
I am Misanthropos, and hate mankind.
For thy part, I do wish thou wert a dog,
That I might love thee something.

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text:
I know thee well;
But in thy fortunes am unlearn'd and strange.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
I know thee too; and more than that I know thee,
I not desire to know. Follow thy drum;
With man's blood paint the ground, gules, gules:
Religious canons, civil laws are cruel;
Then what should war be? This fell whore of thine
Hath in her more destruction than thy sword,
For all her cherubim look.

units.speaker: PHRYNIA
units.text: Thy lips rot off!

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
I will not kiss thee; then the rot returns
To thine own lips again.

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text: How came the noble Timon to this change?

units.speaker: TIMON
ALCIBIADES
units.text:
Noble Timon,
What friendship may I do thee?

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
None, but to
Maintain my opinion.

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text: What is it, Timon?

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Promise me friendship, but perform none: if thou
wilt not promise, the gods plague thee, for thou art
a man! if thou dost perform, confound thee, for
thou art a man!

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text: I have heard in some sort of thy miseries.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Thou saw'st them, when I had prosperity.

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text: I see them now; then was a blessed time.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: As thine is now, held with a brace of harlots.

units.speaker: TIMANDRA
units.text:
Is this the Athenian minion, whom the world
Voiced so regardfully?

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Art thou Timandra?

units.speaker: TIMANDRA
units.text: Yes.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Be a whore still: they love thee not that use thee;
Give them diseases, leaving with thee their lust.
Make use of thy salt hours: season the slaves
For tubs and baths; bring down rose-cheeked youth
To the tub-fast and the diet.

units.speaker: TIMANDRA
units.text: Hang thee, monster!

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text:
Pardon him, sweet Timandra; for his wits
Are drown'd and lost in his calamities.
I have but little gold of late, brave Timon,
The want whereof doth daily make revolt
In my penurious band: I have heard, and grieved,
How cursed Athens, mindless of thy worth,
Forgetting thy great deeds, when neighbour states,
But for thy sword and fortune, trod upon them,—

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: I prithee, beat thy drum, and get thee gone.

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text: I am thy friend, and pity thee, dear Timon.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
How dost thou pity him whom thou dost trouble?
I had rather be alone.

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text:
Why, fare thee well:
Here is some gold for thee.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Keep it, I cannot eat it.

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text: When I have laid proud Athens on a heap,—

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Warr'st thou 'gainst Athens?

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text: Ay, Timon, and have cause.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
The gods confound them all in thy conquest;
And thee after, when thou hast conquer'd!

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text: Why me, Timon?

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
That, by killing of villains,
Thou wast born to conquer my country.
Put up thy gold: go on,—here's gold,—go on;
Be as a planetary plague, when Jove
Will o'er some high-viced city hang his poison
In the sick air: let not thy sword skip one:
Pity not honour'd age for his white beard;
He is an usurer: strike me the counterfeit matron;
It is her habit only that is honest,
Herself's a bawd: let not the virgin's cheek
Make soft thy trenchant sword; for those milk-paps,
That through the window-bars bore at men's eyes,
Are not within the leaf of pity writ,
But set them down horrible traitors: spare not the
babe,
Whose dimpled smiles from fools exhaust their mercy;
Think it a bastard, whom the oracle
Hath doubtfully pronounced thy throat shall cut,
And mince it sans remorse: swear against objects;
Put armour on thine ears and on thine eyes;
Whose proof, nor yells of mothers, maids, nor babes,
Nor sight of priests in holy vestments bleeding,
Shall pierce a jot. There's gold to pay soldiers:
Make large confusion; and, thy fury spent,
Confounded be thyself! Speak not, be gone.

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text:
Hast thou gold yet? I'll take the gold thou
givest me,
Not all thy counsel.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Dost thou, or dost thou not, heaven's curse
upon thee!

units.speaker: TIMANDRA
units.text: Give us some gold, good Timon: hast thou more?

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Enough to make a whore forswear her trade,
And to make whores, a bawd. Hold up, you sluts,
Your aprons mountant: you are not oathable,
Although, I know, you 'll swear, terribly swear
Into strong shudders and to heavenly agues
The immortal gods that hear you, —spare your oaths,
I'll trust to your conditions: be whores still;
And he whose pious breath seeks to convert you,
Be strong in whore, allure him, burn him up;
Let your close fire predominate his smoke,
And be no turncoats: yet may your pains, six months,
Be quite contrary: and thatch your poor thin roofs
With burthens of the dead;—some that were hang'd,
No matter:—wear them, betray with them: whore still;
Paint till a horse may mire upon your face,
A pox of wrinkles!

units.speaker: TIMANDRA
units.text:
Well, more gold: what then?
Believe't, that we'll do any thing for gold.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Consumptions sow
In hollow bones of man; strike their sharp shins,
And mar men's spurring. Crack the lawyer's voice,
That he may never more false title plead,
Nor sound his quillets shrilly: hoar the flamen,
That scolds against the quality of flesh,
And not believes himself: down with the nose,
Down with it flat; take the bridge quite away
Of him that, his particular to foresee,
Smells from the general weal: make curl'd-pate
ruffians bald;
And let the unscarr'd braggarts of the war
Derive some pain from you: plague all;
That your activity may defeat and quell
The source of all erection. There's more gold:
Do you damn others, and let this damn you,
And ditches grave you all!

units.speaker: TIMANDRA
units.text: More counsel with more money, bounteous Timon.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
More whore, more mischief first; I have given you
earnest.

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text:
Strike up the drum towards Athens! Farewell, Timon:
If I thrive well, I'll visit thee again.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: If I hope well, I'll never see thee more.

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text: I never did thee harm.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Yes, thou spokest well of me.

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text: Call'st thou that harm?

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Men daily find it. Get thee away, and take
Thy beagles with thee.

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text: We but offend him. Strike!
Drum beats. Exeunt ALCIBIADES, PHRYNIA, and TIMANDRA

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
That nature, being sick of man's unkindness,
Should yet be hungry! Common mother, thou,

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Whose womb unmeasurable, and infinite breast,
Teems, and feeds all; whose self-same mettle,
Whereof thy proud child, arrogant man, is puff'd,
Engenders the black toad and adder blue,
The gilded newt and eyeless venom'd worm,
With all the abhorred births below crisp heaven
Whereon Hyperion's quickening fire doth shine;
Yield him, who all thy human sons doth hate,
From forth thy plenteous bosom, one poor root!
Ensear thy fertile and conceptious womb,
Let it no more bring out ingrateful man!
Go great with tigers, dragons, wolves, and bears;
Teem with new monsters, whom thy upward face
Hath to the marbled mansion all above
Never presented! —O, a root,—dear thanks!—
Dry up thy marrows, vines, and plough-torn leas;
Whereof ungrateful man, with liquorish draughts
And morsels unctuous, greases his pure mind,
That from it all consideration slips!
Enter APEMANTUS

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: More man? plague, plague!

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text:
I was directed hither: men report
Thou dost affect my manners, and dost use them.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
'Tis, then, because thou dost not keep a dog,
Whom I would imitate: consumption catch thee!

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text:
This is in thee a nature but infected;
A poor unmanly melancholy sprung
From change of fortune. Why this spade? this place?
This slave-like habit? and these looks of care?
Thy flatterers yet wear silk, drink wine, lie soft;
Hug their diseased perfumes, and have forgot
That ever Timon was. Shame not these woods,
By putting on the cunning of a carper.
Be thou a flatterer now, and seek to thrive
By that which has undone thee: hinge thy knee,
And let his very breath, whom thou'lt observe,
Blow off thy cap; praise his most vicious strain,
And call it excellent: thou wast told thus;
Thou gavest thine ears like tapsters that bid welcome
To knaves and all approachers: 'tis most just
That thou turn rascal; hadst thou wealth again,
Rascals should have 't. Do not assume my likeness.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Were I like thee, I'ld throw away myself.

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text:
Thou hast cast away thyself, being like thyself;
A madman so long, now a fool. What, think'st
That the bleak air, thy boisterous chamberlain,
Will put thy shirt on warm? will these moss'd trees,
That have outlived the eagle, page thy heels,
And skip where thou point'st out? will the
cold brook,
Candied with ice, caudle thy morning taste,
To cure thy o'er-night's surfeit? Call the creatures
Whose naked natures live in an the spite
Of wreakful heaven, whose bare unhoused trunks,
To the conflicting elements exposed,
Answer mere nature; bid them flatter thee;
O, thou shalt find—

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: A fool of thee: depart.

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: I love thee better now than e'er I did.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: I hate thee worse.

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: Why?

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Thou flatter'st misery.

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: I flatter not; but say thou art a caitiff.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Why dost thou seek me out?

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: To vex thee.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Always a villain's office or a fool's.
Dost please thyself in't?

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: Ay.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: What! a knave too?

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text:
If thou didst put this sour-cold habit on
To castigate thy pride, 'twere well: but thou
Dost it enforcedly; thou'ldst courtier be again,
Wert thou not beggar. Willing misery
Outlives encertain pomp, is crown'd before:
The one is filling still, never complete;
The other, at high wish: best state, contentless,
Hath a distracted and most wretched being,
Worse than the worst, content.
Thou shouldst desire to die, being miserable.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Not by his breath that is more miserable.
Thou art a slave, whom Fortune's tender arm
With favour never clasp'd; but bred a dog.
Hadst thou, like us from our first swath, proceeded
The sweet degrees that this brief world affords
To such as may the passive drugs of it
Freely command, thou wouldst have plunged thyself
In general riot; melted down thy youth
In different beds of lust; and never learn'd
The icy precepts of respect, but follow'd
The sugar'd game before thee. But myself,
Who had the world as my confectionary,
The mouths, the tongues, the eyes and hearts of men
At duty, more than I could frame employment,
That numberless upon me stuck as leaves
Do on the oak, hive with one winter's brush
Fell from their boughs and left me open, bare
For every storm that blows: I, to bear this,
That never knew but better, is some burden:
Thy nature did commence in sufferance, time
Hath made thee hard in't. Why shouldst thou hate men?
They never flatter'd thee: what hast thou given?
If thou wilt curse, thy father, that poor rag,
Must be thy subject, who in spite put stuff
To some she beggar and compounded thee
Poor rogue hereditary. Hence, be gone!
If thou hadst not been born the worst of men,
Thou hadst been a knave and flatterer.

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: Art thou proud yet?

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Ay, that I am not thee.

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text:
I, that I was
No prodigal.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
I, that I am one now:
Were all the wealth I have shut up in thee,
I'ld give thee leave to hang it. Get thee gone.
That the whole life of Athens were in this!
Thus would I eat it.

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: Here; I will mend thy feast.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: First mend my company, take away thyself.

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: So I shall mend mine own, by the lack of thine.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
'Tis not well mended so, it is but botch'd;
if not, I would it were.

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: What wouldst thou have to Athens?

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Thee thither in a whirlwind. If thou wilt,
Tell them there I have gold; look, so I have.

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: Here is no use for gold.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
The best and truest;
For here it sleeps, and does no hired harm.

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: Where liest o' nights, Timon?

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Under that's above me.
Where feed'st thou o' days, Apemantus?

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text:
Where my stomach finds meat; or, rather, where I eat
it.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Would poison were obedient and knew my mind!

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: Where wouldst thou send it?

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: To sauce thy dishes.

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text:
The middle of humanity thou never knewest, but the
extremity of both ends: when thou wast in thy gilt
and thy perfume, they mocked thee for too much
curiosity; in thy rags thou knowest none, but art
despised for the contrary. There's a medlar for
thee, eat it.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: On what I hate I feed not.

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: Dost hate a medlar?

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Ay, though it look like thee.

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text:
An thou hadst hated meddlers sooner, thou shouldst
have loved thyself better now. What man didst thou
ever know unthrift that was beloved after his means?

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Who, without those means thou talkest of, didst thou
ever know beloved?

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: Myself.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
I understand thee; thou hadst some means to keep a
dog.

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text:
What things in the world canst thou nearest compare
to thy flatterers?

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Women nearest; but men, men are the things
themselves. What wouldst thou do with the world,
Apemantus, if it lay in thy power?

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: Give it the beasts, to be rid of the men.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Wouldst thou have thyself fall in the confusion of
men, and remain a beast with the beasts?

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: Ay, Timon.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
A beastly ambition, which the gods grant thee t'
attain to! If thou wert the lion, the fox would
beguile thee; if thou wert the lamb, the fox would
eat three: if thou wert the fox, the lion would
suspect thee, when peradventure thou wert accused by
the ass: if thou wert the ass, thy dulness would
torment thee, and still thou livedst but as a
breakfast to the wolf: if thou wert the wolf, thy
greediness would afflict thee, and oft thou shouldst
hazard thy life for thy dinner: wert thou the
unicorn, pride and wrath would confound thee and
make thine own self the conquest of thy fury: wert
thou a bear, thou wouldst be killed by the horse:
wert thou a horse, thou wouldst be seized by the
leopard: wert thou a leopard, thou wert german to
the lion and the spots of thy kindred were jurors on
thy life: all thy safety were remotion and thy
defence absence. What beast couldst thou be, that
were not subject to a beast? and what a beast art
thou already, that seest not thy loss in
transformation!

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text:
If thou couldst please me with speaking to me, thou
mightst have hit upon it here: the commonwealth of
Athens is become a forest of beasts.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
How has the ass broke the wall, that thou art out of
the city?

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text:
Yonder comes a poet and a painter: the plague of
company light upon thee! I will fear to catch it
and give way: when I know not what else to do, I'll
see thee again.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
When there is nothing living but thee, thou shalt be
welcome. I had rather be a beggar's dog than Apemantus.

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: Thou art the cap of all the fools alive.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Would thou wert clean enough to spit upon!

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: A plague on thee! thou art too bad to curse.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: All villains that do stand by thee are pure.

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: There is no leprosy but what thou speak'st.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
If I name thee.
I'll beat thee, but I should infect my hands.

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: I would my tongue could rot them off!

units.speaker: TIMON
APEMANTUS
units.text: Would thou wouldst burst!

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Away,
Thou tedious rogue! I am sorry I shall lose
A stone by thee.

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: Beast!

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Slave!

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: Toad!

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Rogue, rogue, rogue!
I am sick of this false world, and will love nought
But even the mere necessities upon 't.
Then, Timon, presently prepare thy grave;
Lie where the light foam the sea may beat
Thy grave-stone daily: make thine epitaph,
That death in me at others' lives may laugh.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
O thou sweet king-killer, and dear divorce
'Twixt natural son and sire! thou bright defiler
Of Hymen's purest bed! thou valiant Mars!
Thou ever young, fresh, loved and delicate wooer,
Whose blush doth thaw the consecrated snow
That lies on Dian's lap! thou visible god,
That solder'st close impossibilities,
And makest them kiss! that speak'st with
every tongue,
To every purpose! O thou touch of hearts!
Think, thy slave man rebels, and by thy virtue
Set them into confounding odds, that beasts
May have the world in empire!

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text:
Would 'twere so!
But not till I am dead. I'll say thou'st gold:
Thou wilt be throng'd to shortly.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Throng'd to!

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: Ay.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Thy back, I prithee.

units.speaker: APEMANTUS
units.text: Live, and love thy misery.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Long live so, and so die.
Exit APEMANTUS

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
I am quit.
Moe things like men! Eat, Timon, and abhor them.
Enter Banditti

units.speaker: FIRST BANDIT
units.text:
Where should he have this gold? It is some poor
fragment, some slender sort of his remainder: the
mere want of gold, and the falling-from of his
friends, drove him into this melancholy.

units.speaker: SECOND BANDIT
units.text: It is noised he hath a mass of treasure.

units.speaker: THIRD BANDIT
units.text:
Let us make the assay upon him: if he care not
for't, he will supply us easily; if he covetously
reserve it, how shall's get it?

units.speaker: SECOND BANDIT
units.text: True; for he bears it not about him, 'tis hid.

units.speaker: FIRST BANDIT
units.text: Is not this he?

units.speaker: BANDITTI
units.text: Where?

units.speaker: SECOND BANDIT
units.text: 'Tis his description.

units.speaker: THIRD BANDIT
units.text: He; I know him.

units.speaker: BANDITTI
units.text: Save thee, Timon.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Now, thieves?

units.speaker: BANDITTI
units.text: Soldiers, not thieves.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Both too; and women's sons.

units.speaker: BANDITTI
units.text: We are not thieves, but men that much do want.

units.speaker: TIMON
FIRST BANDIT
units.text:
We cannot live on grass, on berries, water,
As beasts and birds and fishes.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Nor on the beasts themselves, the birds, and fishes;
You must eat men. Yet thanks I must you con
That you are thieves profess'd, that you work not
In holier shapes: for there is boundless theft
In limited professions. Rascal thieves,
Here's gold. Go, suck the subtle blood o' the grape,
Till the high fever seethe your blood to froth,
And so 'scape hanging: trust not the physician;
His antidotes are poison, and he slays
Moe than you rob: take wealth and lives together;
Do villany, do, since you protest to do't,
Like workmen. I'll example you with thievery.
The sun's a thief, and with his great attraction
Robs the vast sea: the moon's an arrant thief,
And her pale fire she snatches from the sun:
The sea's a thief, whose liquid surge resolves
The moon into salt tears: the earth's a thief,
That feeds and breeds by a composture stolen
From general excrement : each thing's a thief:
The laws, your curb and whip, in their rough power
Have uncheque'd theft. Love not yourselves: away,
Rob one another. There's more gold. Cut throats:
All that you meet are thieves: to Athens go,
Break open shops; nothing can you steal,
But thieves do lose it: steal no less for this
I give you; and gold confound you howsoe'er! Amen.

units.speaker: THIRD BANDIT
units.text:
Has almost charmed me from my profession, by
persuading me to it.

units.speaker: FIRST BANDIT
units.text:
'Tis in the malice of mankind that he thus advises
us; not to have us thrive in our mystery.

units.speaker: SECOND BANDIT
units.text: I'll believe him as an enemy, and give over my trade.

units.speaker: FIRST BANDIT
units.text:
Let us first see peace in Athens: there is no time
so miserable but a man may be true.
Exeunt Banditti
Enter FLAVIUS

units.speaker: FLAVIUS
units.text:
O you gods!
Is yond despised and ruinous man my lord?
Full of decay and failing? O monument
And wonder of good deeds evilly bestow'd!
What an alteration of honour
Has desperate want made!
What viler thing upon the earth than friends
Who can bring noblest minds to basest ends!
How rarely does it meet with this time's guise,
When man was wish'd to love his enemies!
Grant I may ever love, and rather woo
Those that would mischief me than those that do!
Has caught me in his eye: I will present
My honest grief unto him; and, as my lord,
Still serve him with my life. My dearest master!

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Away! what art thou?

units.speaker: FLAVIUS
units.text: Have you forgot me, sir?

units.speaker: TIMON
FLAVIUS
units.text: An honest poor servant of yours.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Then I know thee not:
I never had honest man about me, I; all
I kept were knaves, to serve in meat to villains.

units.speaker: FLAVIUS
units.text:
The gods are witness,
Ne'er did poor steward wear a truer grief
For his undone lord than mine eyes for you.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
What, dost thou weep? Come nearer. Then I
love thee,
Because thou art a woman, and disclaim'st
Flinty mankind; whose eyes do never give
But thorough lust and laughter. Pity's sleeping:
Strange times, that weep with laughing, not with
weeping!

units.speaker: FLAVIUS
units.text:
I beg of you to know me, good my lord,
To accept my grief and whilst this poor wealth lasts
To entertain me as your steward still.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Had I a steward
So true, so just, and now so comfortable?
It almost turns my dangerous nature mild.
Let me behold thy face. Surely, this man
Was born of woman.
Forgive my general and exceptless rashness,
You perpetual-sober gods! I do proclaim
One honest man—mistake me not—but one;
No more, I pray,—and he's a steward.
How fain would I have hated all mankind!
And thou redeem'st thyself: but all, save thee,
I fell with curses.
Methinks thou art more honest now than wise;
For, by oppressing and betraying me,
Thou mightst have sooner got another service:
For many so arrive at second masters,
Upon their first lord's neck. But tell me true—
For I must ever doubt, though ne'er so sure—
Is not thy kindness subtle, covetous,
If not a usuring kindness, and, as rich men deal gifts,
Expecting in return twenty for one?

units.speaker: FLAVIUS
units.text:
No, my most worthy master; in whose breast
Doubt and suspect, alas, are placed too late:
You should have fear'd false times when you did feast:
Suspect still comes where an estate is least.
That which I show, heaven knows, is merely love,
Duty and zeal to your unmatched mind,
Care of your food and living; and, believe it,
My most honour'd lord,
For any benefit that points to me,
Either in hope or present, I'ld exchange
For this one wish, that you had power and wealth
To requite me, by making rich yourself.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Look thee, 'tis so! Thou singly honest man,
Here, take: the gods out of my misery
Have sent thee treasure. Go, live rich and happy;
But thus condition'd: thou shalt build from men;
Hate all, curse all, show charity to none,
But let the famish'd flesh slide from the bone,
Ere thou relieve the beggar; give to dogs
What thou deny'st to men; let prisons swallow 'em,
Debts wither 'em to nothing; be men like
blasted woods,
And may diseases lick up their false bloods!
And so farewell and thrive.

units.speaker: FLAVIUS
units.text:
O, let me stay,
And comfort you, my master.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
If thou hatest curses,
Stay not; fly, whilst thou art blest and free:
Ne'er see thou man, and let me ne'er see thee.
Exit FLAVIUS. TIMON retires to his cave
Enter POET and PAINTER; TIMON watching them from his cave

units.speaker: PAINTER
units.text:
As I took note of the place, it cannot be far where
he abides.

units.speaker: POET
units.text:
What's to be thought of him? does the rumour hold
for true, that he's so full of gold?

units.speaker: PAINTER
units.text:
Certain: Alcibiades reports it; Phrynia and
Timandra had gold of him: he likewise enriched poor
straggling soldiers with great quantity: 'tis said
he gave unto his steward a mighty sum.

units.speaker: POET
units.text:
Then this breaking of his has been but a try for his
friends.

units.speaker: PAINTER
units.text:
Nothing else: you shall see him a palm in Athens
again, and flourish with the highest. Therefore
'tis not amiss we tender our loves to him, in this
supposed distress of his: it will show honestly in
us; and is very likely to load our purposes with
what they travail for, if it be a just true report
that goes of his having.

units.speaker: POET
units.text: What have you now to present unto him?

units.speaker: PAINTER
units.text:
Nothing at this time but my visitation: only I will
promise him an excellent piece.

units.speaker: POET
units.text:
I must serve him so too, tell him of an intent
that's coming toward him.

units.speaker: PAINTER
units.text:
Good as the best. Promising is the very air o' the
time: it opens the eyes of expectation:
performance is ever the duller for his act; and,
but in the plainer and simpler kind of people, the
deed of saying is quite out of use. To promise is
most courtly and fashionable: performance is a kind
of will or testament which argues a great sickness
in his judgment that makes it.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
[Aside] Excellent workman! thou canst not paint a
man so bad as is thyself.

units.speaker: POET
units.text:
I am thinking what I shall say I have provided for
him: it must be a personating of himself; a satire
against the softness of prosperity, with a discovery
of the infinite flatteries that follow youth and
opulency.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
[Aside] Must thou needs stand for a villain in
thine own work? wilt thou whip thine own faults in
other men? Do so, I have gold for thee.

units.speaker: POET
units.text:
Nay, let's seek him:
Then do we sin against our own estate,
When we may profit meet, and come too late.

units.speaker: PAINTER
TIMON
units.text:
[Aside] I'll meet you at the turn. What a
god's gold,
That he is worshipp'd in a baser temple
Than where swine feed!
'Tis thou that rigg'st the bark and plough'st the foam,
Settlest admired reverence in a slave:
To thee be worship! and thy saints for aye
Be crown'd with plagues that thee alone obey!
Fit I meet them.

units.speaker: POET
units.text: Hail, worthy Timon!

units.speaker: PAINTER
units.text: Our late noble master!

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Have I once lived to see two honest men?

units.speaker: POET
units.text:
Sir,
Having often of your open bounty tasted,
Hearing you were retired, your friends fall'n off,
Whose thankless natures—O abhorred spirits!—
Not all the whips of heaven are large enough:
What! to you,
Whose star-like nobleness gave life and influence
To their whole being! I am rapt and cannot cover
The monstrous bulk of this ingratitude
With any size of words.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Let it go naked, men may see't the better:
You that are honest, by being what you are,
Make them best seen and known.

units.speaker: PAINTER
units.text:
He and myself
Have travail'd in the great shower of your gifts,
And sweetly felt it.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Ay, you are honest men.

units.speaker: PAINTER
units.text: We are hither come to offer you our service.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Most honest men! Why, how shall I requite you?
Can you eat roots, and drink cold water? no.

units.speaker: BOTH
units.text: What we can do, we'll do, to do you service.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Ye're honest men: ye've heard that I have gold;
I am sure you have: speak truth; ye're honest men.

units.speaker: PAINTER
units.text:
So it is said, my noble lord; but therefore
Came not my friend nor I.

units.speaker: TIMON
PAINTER
units.text: So, so, my lord.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
E'en so, sir, as I say. And, for thy fiction,
Why, thy verse swells with stuff so fine and smooth
That thou art even natural in thine art.
But, for all this, my honest-natured friends,
I must needs say you have a little fault:
Marry, 'tis not monstrous in you, neither wish I
You take much pains to mend.

units.speaker: BOTH
units.text:
Beseech your honour
To make it known to us.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: You'll take it ill.

units.speaker: BOTH
units.text: Most thankfully, my lord.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Will you, indeed?

units.speaker: BOTH
units.text: Doubt it not, worthy lord.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
There's never a one of you but trusts a knave,
That mightily deceives you.

units.speaker: BOTH
units.text: Do we, my lord?

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Ay, and you hear him cog, see him dissemble,
Know his gross patchery, love him, feed him,
Keep in your bosom: yet remain assured
That he's a made-up villain.

units.speaker: PAINTER
units.text: I know none such, my lord.

units.speaker: POET
units.text: Nor I.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Look you, I love you well; I'll give you gold,
Rid me these villains from your companies:
Hang them or stab them, drown them in a draught,
Confound them by some course, and come to me,
I'll give you gold enough.

units.speaker: BOTH
units.text: Name them, my lord, let's know them.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
You that way and you this, but two in company;
Each man apart, all single and alone,
Yet an arch-villain keeps him company.
If where thou art two villains shall not be,
Come not near him. If thou wouldst not reside
But where one villain is, then him abandon.
Hence, pack! there's gold; you came for gold, ye
slaves:

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: You have work'd for me; there's payment for you: hence!

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
You are an alchemist; make gold of that.
Out, rascal dogs!
Enter FLAVIUS and two Senators

units.speaker: FLAVIUS
units.text:
It is in vain that you would speak with Timon;
For he is set so only to himself
That nothing but himself which looks like man
Is friendly with him.

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text:
Bring us to his cave:
It is our part and promise to the Athenians
To speak with Timon.

units.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
units.text:
At all times alike
Men are not still the same: 'twas time and griefs
That framed him thus: time, with his fairer hand,
Offering the fortunes of his former days,
The former man may make him. Bring us to him,
And chance it as it may.

units.speaker: FLAVIUS
units.text:
Here is his cave.
Peace and content be here! Lord Timon! Timon!
Look out, and speak to friends: the Athenians,
By two of their most reverend senate, greet thee:
Speak to them, noble Timon.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Thou sun, that comfort'st, burn! Speak, and
be hang'd:
For each true word, a blister! and each false
Be as cauterizing to the root o' the tongue,
Consuming it with speaking!

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text: Worthy Timon,—

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Of none but such as you, and you of Timon.

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text: The senators of Athens greet thee, Timon.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
I thank them; and would send them back the plague,
Could I but catch it for them.

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text:
O, forget
What we are sorry for ourselves in thee.
The senators with one consent of love
Entreat thee back to Athens; who have thought
On special dignities, which vacant lie
For thy best use and wearing.

units.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
units.text:
They confess
Toward thee forgetfulness too general, gross:
Which now the public body, which doth seldom
Play the recanter, feeling in itself
A lack of Timon's aid, hath sense withal
Of its own fail, restraining aid to Timon;
And send forth us, to make their sorrow'd render,
Together with a recompense more fruitful
Than their offence can weigh down by the dram;
Ay, even such heaps and sums of love and wealth
As shall to thee blot out what wrongs were theirs
And write in thee the figures of their love,
Ever to read them thine.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
You witch me in it;
Surprise me to the very brink of tears:
Lend me a fool's heart and a woman's eyes,
And I'll beweep these comforts, worthy senators.

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text:
Therefore, so please thee to return with us
And of our Athens, thine and ours, to take
The captainship, thou shalt be met with thanks,
Allow'd with absolute power and thy good name
Live with authority: so soon we shall drive back
Of Alcibiades the approaches wild,
Who, like a boar too savage, doth root up
His country's peace.

units.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
units.text:
And shakes his threatening sword
Against the walls of Athens.

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text: Therefore, Timon,—

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Well, sir, I will; therefore, I will, sir; thus:
If Alcibiades kill my countrymen,
Let Alcibiades know this of Timon,
That Timon cares not. But if be sack fair Athens,
And take our goodly aged men by the beards,
Giving our holy virgins to the stain
Of contumelious, beastly, mad-brain'd war,
Then let him know, and tell him Timon speaks it,
In pity of our aged and our youth,
I cannot choose but tell him, that I care not,
And let him take't at worst; for their knives care not,
While you have throats to answer: for myself,
There's not a whittle in the unruly camp
But I do prize it at my love before
The reverend'st throat in Athens. So I leave you
To the protection of the prosperous gods,
As thieves to keepers.

units.speaker: FLAVIUS
units.text: Stay not, all's in vain.

units.speaker: TIMON
FIRST SENATOR
units.text: We speak in vain.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
But yet I love my country, and am not
One that rejoices in the common wreck,
As common bruit doth put it.

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text: That's well spoke.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text: Commend me to my loving countrymen,—

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text:
These words become your lips as they pass
thorough them.

And enter in our ears like great triumphers
In their applauding gates.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Commend me to them,
And tell them that, to ease them of their griefs,
Their fears of hostile strokes, their aches, losses,
Their pangs of love, with other incident throes
That nature's fragile vessel doth sustain
In life's uncertain voyage, I will some kindness do
them:
I'll teach them to prevent wild Alcibiades' wrath.

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text: I like this well; he will return again.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
I have a tree, which grows here in my close,
That mine own use invites me to cut down,
And shortly must I fell it: tell my friends,
Tell Athens, in the sequence of degree
From high to low throughout, that whoso please
To stop affliction, let him take his haste,
Come hither, ere my tree hath felt the axe,
And hang himself. I pray you, do my greeting.

units.speaker: FLAVIUS
units.text: Trouble him no further; thus you still shall find him.

units.speaker: TIMON
units.text:
Come not to me again: but say to Athens,
Timon hath made his everlasting mansion
Upon the beached verge of the salt flood;
Who once a day with his embossed froth
The turbulent surge shall cover: thither come,
And let my grave-stone be your oracle.
Lips, let sour words go by and language end:
What is amiss plague and infection mend!
Graves only be men's works and death their gain!
Sun, hide thy beams! Timon hath done his reign.
Retires to his cave

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text:
His discontents are unremoveably
Coupled to nature.

units.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
units.text:
Our hope in him is dead: let us return,
And strain what other means is left unto us
In our dear peril.

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text: It requires swift foot.
Exeunt
Enter two Senators and a Messenger

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text:
Thou hast painfully discover'd: are his files
As full as thy report?

units.speaker: MESSENGER
units.text:
I have spoke the least:
Besides, his expedition promises
Present approach.

units.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
units.text: We stand much hazard, if they bring not Timon.

units.speaker: MESSENGER
units.text:
I met a courier, one mine ancient friend;
Whom, though in general part we were opposed,
Yet our old love made a particular force,
And made us speak like friends: this man was riding
From Alcibiades to Timon's cave,
With letters of entreaty, which imported
His fellowship i' the cause against your city,
In part for his sake moved.

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text: Here come our brothers.
Enter the Senators from TIMON

units.speaker: THIRD SENATOR
units.text:
No talk of Timon, nothing of him expect.
The enemies' drum is heard, and fearful scouring
Doth choke the air with dust: in, and prepare:
Ours is the fall, I fear; our foes the snare.
Exeunt
Enter a Soldier, seeking TIMON

units.speaker: SOLDIER
units.text:
By all description this should be the place.
Who's here? speak, ho! No answer! What is this?
Timon is dead, who hath outstretch'd his span:
Some beast rear'd this; there does not live a man.
Dead, sure; and this his grave. What's on this tomb
I cannot read; the character I'll take with wax:
Our captain hath in every figure skill,
An aged interpreter, though young in days:
Before proud Athens he's set down by this,
Whose fall the mark of his ambition is.
Exit
Trumpets sound. Enter ALCIBIADES with his powers

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text:
Sound to this coward and lascivious town
Our terrible approach.
A parley sounded
Enter Senators on the walls

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text:
Till now you have gone on and fill'd the time
With all licentious measure, making your wills
The scope of justice; till now myself and such
As slept within the shadow of your power
Have wander'd with our traversed arms and breathed
Our sufferance vainly: now the time is flush,
When crouching marrow in the bearer strong
Cries of itself 'No more:' now breathless wrong
Shall sit and pant in your great chairs of ease,
And pursy insolence shall break his wind
With fear and horrid flight.

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text:
Noble and young,
When thy first griefs were but a mere conceit,
Ere thou hadst power or we had cause of fear,
We sent to thee, to give thy rages balm,
To wipe out our ingratitude with loves
Above their quantity.

units.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
units.text:
So did we woo
Transformed Timon to our city's love
By humble message and by promised means:
We were not all unkind, nor all deserve
The common stroke of war.

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text:
These walls of ours
Were not erected by their hands from whom
You have received your griefs; nor are they such
That these great towers, trophies and schools
should fall
For private faults in them.

units.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
units.text:
Nor are they living
Who were the motives that you first went out;
Shame that they wanted cunning, in excess
Hath broke their hearts. March, noble lord,
Into our city with thy banners spread:
By decimation, and a tithed death—
If thy revenges hunger for that food
Which nature loathes—take thou the destined tenth,
And by the hazard of the spotted die
Let die the spotted.

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text:
All have not offended;
For those that were, it is not square to take
On those that are, revenges: crimes, like lands,
Are not inherited. Then, dear countryman,
Bring in thy ranks, but leave without thy rage:
Spare thy Athenian cradle and those kin
Which in the bluster of thy wrath must fall
With those that have offended: like a shepherd,
Approach the fold and cull the infected forth,
But kill not all together.

units.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
units.text:
What thou wilt,
Thou rather shalt enforce it with thy smile
Than hew to't with thy sword.

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text:
Set but thy foot
Against our rampired gates, and they shall ope;
So thou wilt send thy gentle heart before,
To say thou'lt enter friendly.

units.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
units.text:
Throw thy glove,
Or any token of thine honour else,
That thou wilt use the wars as thy redress
And not as our confusion, all thy powers
Shall make their harbour in our town, till we
Have seal'd thy full desire.

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text:
Then there's my glove;
Descend, and open your uncharged ports:
Those enemies of Timon's and mine own
Whom you yourselves shall set out for reproof
Fall and no more: and, to atone your fears
With my more noble meaning, not a man
Shall pass his quarter, or offend the stream
Of regular justice in your city's bounds,
But shall be render'd to your public laws
At heaviest answer.

units.speaker: BOTH
units.text: 'Tis most nobly spoken.

units.speaker: ALCIBIADES
units.text: Descend, and keep your words.
Enter Soldier

units.speaker: SOLDIER
ALCIBIADES
units.text:
[Reads the epitaph] 'Here lies a
wretched corse, of wretched soul bereft:
Seek not my name: a plague consume you wicked
caitiffs left!
Here lie I, Timon; who, alive, all living men did hate:
Pass by and curse thy fill, but pass and stay
not here thy gait.'
These well express in thee thy latter spirits:
Though thou abhorr'dst in us our human griefs,
Scorn'dst our brain's flow and those our
droplets which
From niggard nature fall, yet rich conceit
Taught thee to make vast Neptune weep for aye
On thy low grave, on faults forgiven. Dead
Is noble Timon: of whose memory
Hereafter more. Bring me into your city,
And I will use the olive with my sword,
Make war breed peace, make peace stint war, make each
Prescribe to other as each other's leech.
Let our drums strike.
Exeunt

sentences.text: Enter Poet, Painter, Jeweller, Merchant, and others, at several doors

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text: Good day, sir.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text: I am glad you're well.

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text: I have not seen you long: how goes the world?

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text: I have not seen you long: how goes the world?

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text: It wears, sir, as it grows.

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text:
Ay, that's well known:
But what particular rarity? what strange,
Which manifold record not matches? See,
Magic of bounty! all these spirits thy power
Hath conjured to attend. I know the merchant.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text: I know them both; th' other's a jeweller.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text: I know them both; th' other's a jeweller.

sentences.speaker: MERCHANT
sentences.text: O, 'tis a worthy lord.

sentences.speaker: JEWELLER
sentences.text: Nay, that's most fix'd.

sentences.speaker: MERCHANT
sentences.text:
A most incomparable man, breathed, as it were,
To an untirable and continuate goodness:
He passes.

sentences.speaker: MERCHANT
sentences.text:
A most incomparable man, breathed, as it were,

He passes.

sentences.speaker: JEWELLER
sentences.text: I have a jewel here—

sentences.speaker: MERCHANT
sentences.text: O, pray, let's see't: for the Lord Timon, sir?

sentences.speaker: MERCHANT
sentences.text: O, pray, let's see't: for the Lord Timon, sir?

sentences.speaker: JEWELLER
sentences.text: If he will touch the estimate: but, for that—

sentences.speaker: JEWELLER
sentences.text: If he will touch the estimate: but, for that—

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text:
[Reciting to himself] 'When we for recompense have
praised the vile,
It stains the glory in that happy verse
Which aptly sings the good.'

sentences.speaker: MERCHANT
sentences.text: 'Tis a good form.

sentences.speaker: JEWELLER
sentences.text: And rich: here is a water, look ye.

sentences.speaker: JEWELLER
sentences.text: And rich: here is a water, look ye.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text:
You are rapt, sir, in some work, some dedication
To the great lord.

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text:
A thing slipp'd idly from me.
Our poesy is as a gum, which oozes
From whence 'tis nourish'd: the fire i' the flint
Shows not till it be struck; our gentle flame
Provokes itself and like the current flies
Each bound it chafes. What have you there?

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text: A picture, sir. When comes your book forth?

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text:
Upon the heels of my presentment, sir.
Let's see your piece.

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text:
Upon the heels of my presentment, sir.
Let's see your piece.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text: 'Tis a good piece.

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text: So 'tis: this comes off well and excellent.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text: Indifferent.

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text:
Admirable: how this grace
Speaks his own standing! what a mental power
This eye shoots forth! how big imagination
Moves in this lip! to the dumbness of the gesture
One might interpret.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text:
It is a pretty mocking of the life.
Here is a touch; is't good?

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text:
It is a pretty mocking of the life.
Here is a touch; is't good?

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text:
I will say of it,
It tutors nature: artificial strife
Lives in these touches, livelier than life.

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text:
I will say of it,
It tutors nature: artificial strife

Enter certain Senators, and pass over

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text: How this lord is follow'd!

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text: The senators of Athens: happy man!

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text: The senators of Athens: happy man!

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text: Look, more!

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text:
You see this confluence, this great flood
of visitors.
I have, in this rough work, shaped out a man,
Whom this beneath world doth embrace and hug
With amplest entertainment: my free drift
Halts not particularly, but moves itself
In a wide sea of wax: no levell'd malice
Infects one comma in the course I hold;
But flies an eagle flight, bold and forth on,
Leaving no tract behind.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text: How shall I understand you?

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text:
I will unbolt to you.
You see how all conditions, how all minds,
As well of glib and slippery creatures as
Of grave and austere quality, tender down
Their services to Lord Timon: his large fortune
Upon his good and gracious nature hanging
Subdues and properties to his love and tendance
All sorts of hearts; yea, from the glass-faced
flatterer
To Apemantus, that few things loves better
Than to abhor himself: even he drops down
The knee before him, and returns in peace
Most rich in Timon's nod.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text: I saw them speak together.

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text:
Sir, I have upon a high and pleasant hill
Feign'd Fortune to be throned: the base o' the mount
Is rank'd with all deserts, all kind of natures,
That labour on the bosom of this sphere
To propagate their states: amongst them all,
Whose eyes are on this sovereign lady fix'd,
One do I personate of Lord Timon's frame,
Whom Fortune with her ivory hand wafts to her;
Whose present grace to present slaves and servants
Translates his rivals.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text:
'Tis conceived to scope.
This throne, this Fortune, and this hill, methinks,
With one man beckon'd from the rest below,
Bowing his head against the sleepy mount
To climb his happiness, would be well express'd
In our condition.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text:
Nay, sir, but hear me on.
All those which were his fellows but of late,
Some better than his value, on the moment
Follow his strides, his lobbies fill with tendance,
Rain sacrificial whisperings in his ear,
Make sacred even his stirrup, and through him
Drink the free air.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text: Ay, marry, what of these?

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text:
When Fortune in her shift and change of mood
Spurns down her late beloved, all his dependants
Which labour'd after him to the mountain's top
Even on their knees and hands, let him slip down,
Not one accompanying his declining foot.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text:
'Tis common:
A thousand moral paintings I can show
That shall demonstrate these quick blows of Fortune's
More pregnantly than words. Yet you do well
To show Lord Timon that mean eyes have seen
The foot above the head.

Trumpets sound. Enter TIMON, addressing himself courteously to every suitor; a Messenger from VENTIDIUS talking with him; LUCILIUS and other servants following

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Imprison'd is he, say you?

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
Ay, my good lord: five talents is his debt,
His means most short, his creditors most strait:
Your honourable letter he desires
To those have shut him up; which failing,
Periods his comfort.

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
Ay, my good lord: five talents is his debt,
His means most short, his creditors most strait:
Your honourable letter he desires
To those have shut him up; which failing,
Periods his comfort.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Noble Ventidius! Well;
I am not of that feather to shake off
My friend when he must need me. I do know him
A gentleman that well deserves a help:
Which he shall have: I'll pay the debt,
and free him.

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text: Your lordship ever binds him.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Commend me to him: I will send his ransom;
And being enfranchised, bid him come to me.
'Tis not enough to help the feeble up,
But to support him after. Fare you well.

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text: All happiness to your honour!

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER

Exit
Enter an old Athenian

sentences.speaker: OLD ATHENIAN
sentences.text: Lord Timon, hear me speak.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Freely, good father.

sentences.speaker: OLD ATHENIAN
sentences.text: Thou hast a servant named Lucilius.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: I have so: what of him?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: I have so: what of him?

sentences.speaker: OLD ATHENIAN
sentences.text: Most noble Timon, call the man before thee.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Attends he here, or no? Lucilius!

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Attends he here, or no? Lucilius!

sentences.speaker: LUCILIUS
sentences.text: Here, at your lordship's service.

sentences.speaker: OLD ATHENIAN
sentences.text:
This fellow here, Lord Timon, this thy creature,
By night frequents my house. I am a man
That from my first have been inclined to thrift;
And my estate deserves an heir more raised
Than one which holds a trencher.

sentences.speaker: OLD ATHENIAN
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Well; what further?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Well; what further?

sentences.speaker: OLD ATHENIAN
sentences.text:
One only daughter have I, no kin else,
On whom I may confer what I have got:
The maid is fair, o' the youngest for a bride,
And I have bred her at my dearest cost
In qualities of the best. This man of thine
Attempts her love: I prithee, noble lord,
Join with me to forbid him her resort;
Myself have spoke in vain.

sentences.speaker: OLD ATHENIAN
sentences.text:
One only daughter have I, no kin else,
On whom I may confer what I have got:
The maid is fair, o' the youngest for a bride,
And I have bred her at my dearest cost
In qualities of the best. This man of thine
Attempts her love: I prithee, noble lord,
Join with me to forbid him her resort;
Myself have spoke in vain.

sentences.text: The man is honest.

sentences.speaker: OLD ATHENIAN
OLD ATHENIAN
OLD ATHENIAN
TIMON
sentences.text: Does she love him?

sentences.speaker: OLD ATHENIAN
sentences.text:
She is young and apt:
Our own precedent passions do instruct us
What levity's in youth.

sentences.speaker: OLD ATHENIAN
sentences.text:
She is young and apt:
Our own precedent passions do instruct us
What levity's in youth.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: [To LUCILIUS] Love you the maid?

sentences.speaker: LUCILIUS
sentences.text: Ay, my good lord, and she accepts of it.

sentences.speaker: OLD ATHENIAN
sentences.text:
If in her marriage my consent be missing,
I call the gods to witness, I will choose
Mine heir from forth the beggars of the world,
And dispossess her all.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
How shall she be endow'd,
if she be mated with an equal husband?

sentences.speaker: OLD ATHENIAN
sentences.text: Three talents on the present; in future, all.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
This gentleman of mine hath served me long:
To build his fortune I will strain a little,
For 'tis a bond in men. Give him thy daughter:
What you bestow, in him I'll counterpoise,
And make him weigh with her.

sentences.speaker: OLD ATHENIAN
sentences.text:
Most noble lord,
Pawn me to this your honour, she is his.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: My hand to thee; mine honour on my promise.

sentences.speaker: LUCILIUS
sentences.text:
Humbly I thank your lordship: never may
The state or fortune fall into my keeping,
Which is not owed to you!

sentences.speaker: LUCILIUS
sentences.text:
Humbly I thank your lordship: never may
The state or fortune fall into my keeping,
Which is not owed to you!
Exeunt LUCILIUS and Old Athenian

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text: Vouchsafe my labour, and long live your lordship!

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
I thank you; you shall hear from me anon:
Go not away. What have you there, my friend?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
I thank you; you shall hear from me anon:
Go not away. What have you there, my friend?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
I thank you; you shall hear from me anon:
Go not away. What have you there, my friend?

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text:
A piece of painting, which I do beseech
Your lordship to accept.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Painting is welcome.
The painting is almost the natural man;
or since dishonour traffics with man's nature,
He is but outside: these pencill'd figures are
Even such as they give out. I like your work;
And you shall find I like it: wait attendance
Till you hear further from me.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
TIMON
TIMON
JEWELLER
sentences.text: What, my lord! dispraise?

sentences.speaker: JEWELLER
sentences.text: What, my lord! dispraise?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
A more satiety of commendations.
If I should pay you for't as 'tis extoll'd,
It would unclew me quite.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
A more satiety of commendations.
If I should pay you for't as 'tis extoll'd,
It would unclew me quite.

sentences.speaker: JEWELLER
sentences.text:
My lord, 'tis rated
As those which sell would give: but you well know,
Things of like value differing in the owners
Are prized by their masters: believe't, dear lord,
You mend the jewel by the wearing it.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Well mock'd.

sentences.speaker: MERCHANT
sentences.text:
No, my good lord; he speaks the common tongue,
Which all men speak with him.

sentences.speaker: MERCHANT
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Look, who comes here: will you be chid?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Look, who comes here: will you be chid?
Enter APEMANTUS

sentences.speaker: JEWELLER
sentences.text: We'll bear, with your lordship.

sentences.speaker: MERCHANT
sentences.text: He'll spare none.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Good morrow to thee, gentle Apemantus!

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
Till I be gentle, stay thou for thy good morrow;
When thou art Timon's dog, and these knaves honest.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Why dost thou call them knaves? thou know'st them not.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Why dost thou call them knaves? thou know'st them not.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Are they not Athenians?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Yes.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Then I repent not.

sentences.speaker: JEWELLER
sentences.text: You know me, Apemantus?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Thou know'st I do: I call'd thee by thy name.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Thou art proud, Apemantus.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Of nothing so much as that I am not like Timon.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Whither art going?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: To knock out an honest Athenian's brains.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: That's a deed thou'lt die for.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Right, if doing nothing be death by the law.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: How likest thou this picture, Apemantus?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: The best, for the innocence.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Wrought he not well that painted it?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
He wrought better that made the painter; and yet
he's but a filthy piece of work.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text: You're a dog.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
Thy mother's of my generation: what's she, if I be a
dog?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Wilt dine with me, Apemantus?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: No; I eat not lords.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: An thou shouldst, thou 'ldst anger ladies.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: O, they eat lords; so they come by great bellies.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: That's a lascivious apprehension.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: So thou apprehendest it: take it for thy labour.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: How dost thou like this jewel, Apemantus?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
Not so well as plain-dealing, which will not cost a
man a doit.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: What dost thou think 'tis worth?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Not worth my thinking. How now, poet!

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Not worth my thinking. How now, poet!

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text: How now, philosopher!

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Thou liest.

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text: Art not one?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Yes.

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text: Then I lie not.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Art not a poet?

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text: Yes.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
Then thou liest: look in thy last work, where thou
hast feigned him a worthy fellow.

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text: That's not feigned; he is so.

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text: That's not feigned; he is so.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
Yes, he is worthy of thee, and to pay thee for thy
labour: he that loves to be flattered is worthy o'
the flatterer. Heavens, that I were a lord!

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
Yes, he is worthy of thee, and to pay thee for thy
labour: he that loves to be flattered is worthy o'
the flatterer. Heavens, that I were a lord!

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:

labour: he that loves to be flattered is worthy o'
the flatterer. Heavens, that I were a lord!

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: What wouldst do then, Apemantus?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: E'en as Apemantus does now; hate a lord with my heart.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: What, thyself?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: What, thyself?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Ay.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Wherefore?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
That I had no angry wit to be a lord.
Art not thou a merchant?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
That I had no angry wit to be a lord.
Art not thou a merchant?

sentences.speaker: MERCHANT
sentences.text: Ay, Apemantus.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Traffic confound thee, if the gods will not!

sentences.speaker: MERCHANT
sentences.text: If traffic do it, the gods do it.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Traffic's thy god; and thy god confound thee!
Trumpet sounds. Enter a Messenger
Trumpet sounds. Enter a Messenger

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: What trumpet's that?

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
'Tis Alcibiades, and some twenty horse,
All of companionship.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Pray, entertain them; give them guide to us.
Exeunt some Attendants

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
You must needs dine with me: go not you hence
Till I have thank'd you: when dinner's done,
Show me this piece. I am joyful of your sights.

sentences.speaker: TIMON

Till I have thank'd you: when dinner's done,

Enter ALCIBIADES, with the rest

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Most welcome, sir!

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
So, so, there!
Aches contract and starve your supple joints!
That there should be small love 'mongst these
sweet knaves,
And all this courtesy! The strain of man's bred out
Into baboon and monkey.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS

sweet knaves,
And all this courtesy! The strain of man's bred out

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text:
Sir, you have saved my longing, and I feed
Most hungerly on your sight.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Right welcome, sir!
Ere we depart, we'll share a bounteous time
In different pleasures. Pray you, let us in.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:

Exeunt all except APEMANTUS
Enter two Lords

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD
sentences.text: What time o' day is't, Apemantus?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Time to be honest.

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD
sentences.text: That time serves still.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: The more accursed thou, that still omitt'st it.

sentences.speaker: SECOND LORD
sentences.text: Thou art going to Lord Timon's feast?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Ay, to see meat fill knaves and wine heat fools.

sentences.speaker: SECOND LORD
sentences.text: Fare thee well, fare thee well.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Thou art a fool to bid me farewell twice.

sentences.speaker: SECOND LORD
sentences.text: Why, Apemantus?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
Shouldst have kept one to thyself, for I mean to
give thee none.

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD
sentences.text: Hang thyself!

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
No, I will do nothing at thy bidding: make thy
requests to thy friend.

sentences.speaker: SECOND LORD
sentences.text: Away, unpeaceable dog, or I'll spurn thee hence!

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: I will fly, like a dog, the heels o' the ass.
Exit

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD
sentences.text:
He's opposite to humanity. Come, shall we in,
And taste Lord Timon's bounty? he outgoes
The very heart of kindness.

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD

And taste Lord Timon's bounty? he outgoes

sentences.speaker: SECOND LORD
sentences.text:
He pours it out; Plutus, the god of gold,
Is but his steward: no meed, but he repays
Sevenfold above itself; no gift to him,
But breeds the giver a return exceeding
All use of quittance.

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD
sentences.text:
The noblest mind he carries
That ever govern'd man.

sentences.speaker: SECOND LORD
sentences.text: Long may he live in fortunes! Shall we in?

sentences.speaker: SECOND LORD
sentences.text: Long may he live in fortunes! Shall we in?

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD
sentences.text: I'll keep you company.

sentences.speaker: VENTIDIUS
sentences.text:
Most honour'd Timon,
It hath pleased the gods to remember my father's age,
And call him to long peace.
He is gone happy, and has left me rich:
Then, as in grateful virtue I am bound
To your free heart, I do return those talents,
Doubled with thanks and service, from whose help
I derived liberty.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
O, by no means,
Honest Ventidius; you mistake my love:
I gave it freely ever; and there's none
Can truly say he gives, if he receives:
If our betters play at that game, we must not dare
To imitate them; faults that are rich are fair.

sentences.speaker: VENTIDIUS
sentences.text: A noble spirit!

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Nay, my lords,
They all stand ceremoniously looking on TIMON

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Ceremony was but devised at first
To set a gloss on faint deeds, hollow welcomes,
Recanting goodness, sorry ere 'tis shown;
But where there is true friendship, there needs none.
Pray, sit; more welcome are ye to my fortunes
Than my fortunes to me.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Ho, ho, confess'd it! hang'd it, have you not?

sentences.text:
Fie, thou'rt a churl; ye've got a humour there
Does not become a man: 'tis much to blame.
They say, my lords, 'ira furor brevis est;' but yond
man is ever angry. Go, let him have a table by
himself, for he does neither affect company, nor is
he fit for't, indeed.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
Let me stay at thine apperil, Timon: I come to
observe; I give thee warning on't.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
Let me stay at thine apperil, Timon: I come to
observe; I give thee warning on't.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
TIMON
TIMON
APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
I scorn thy meat; 'twould choke me, for I should
ne'er flatter thee. O you gods, what a number of
men eat Timon, and he sees 'em not! It grieves me
to see so many dip their meat in one man's blood;
and all the madness is, he cheers them up too.
I wonder men dare trust themselves with men:
Methinks they should invite them without knives;
Good for their meat, and safer for their lives.
There's much example for't; the fellow that sits
next him now, parts bread with him, pledges the
breath of him in a divided draught, is the readiest
man to kill him: 't has been proved. If I were a
huge man, I should fear to drink at meals;
Lest they should spy my windpipe's dangerous notes:
Great men should drink with harness on their throats.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: My lord, in heart; and let the health go round.

sentences.speaker: SECOND LORD
sentences.text: Let it flow this way, my good lord.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
Flow this way! A brave fellow! he keeps his tides
well. Those healths will make thee and thy state
look ill, Timon. Here's that which is too weak to
be a sinner, honest water, which ne'er left man i' the
mire:
This and my food are equals; there's no odds:
Feasts are too proud to give thanks to the gods.
[Apemantus' Grace]
Immortal gods, I crave no pelf;
I pray for no man but myself:
Grant I may never prove so fond,
To trust man on his oath or bond;
Or a harlot, for her weeping;
Or a dog, that seems a-sleeping:
Or a keeper with my freedom;
Or my friends, if I should need 'em.
Amen. So fall to't:
Rich men sin, and I eat root.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Much good dich thy good heart, Apemantus!

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Captain Alcibiades, your heart's in the field now.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text: My heart is ever at your service, my lord.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
You had rather be at a breakfast of enemies than a
dinner of friends.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text:
So the were bleeding-new, my lord, there's no meat
like 'em: I could wish my best friend at such a feast.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text:
So the were bleeding-new, my lord, there's no meat
like 'em: I could wish my best friend at such a feast.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
Would all those fatterers were thine enemies then,
that then thou mightst kill 'em and bid me to 'em!

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD
sentences.text:
Might we but have that happiness, my lord, that you
would once use our hearts, whereby we might express
some part of our zeals, we should think ourselves
for ever perfect.

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD
sentences.text:
Might we but have that happiness, my lord, that you
would once use our hearts, whereby we might express
some part of our zeals, we should think ourselves
for ever perfect.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
O, no doubt, my good friends, but the gods
themselves have provided that I shall have much help
from you: how had you been my friends else? why
have you that charitable title from thousands, did
not you chiefly belong to my heart? I have told
more of you to myself than you can with modesty
speak in your own behalf; and thus far I confirm
you. O you gods, think I, what need we have any
friends, if we should ne'er have need of 'em? they
were the most needless creatures living, should we
ne'er have use for 'em, and would most resemble
sweet instruments hung up in cases that keep their
sounds to themselves. Why, I have often wished
myself poorer, that I might come nearer to you. We
are born to do benefits: and what better or
properer can we can our own than the riches of our
friends? O, what a precious comfort 'tis, to have
so many, like brothers, commanding one another's
fortunes! O joy, e'en made away ere 't can be born!
Mine eyes cannot hold out water, methinks: to
forget their faults, I drink to you.

have you that charitable title from thousands, did
not you chiefly belong to my heart? I have told
more of you to myself than you can with modesty
speak in your own behalf; and thus far I confirm
you. O you gods, think I, what need we have any
friends, if we should ne'er have need of 'em? they
were the most needless creatures living, should we
ne'er have use for 'em, and would most resemble
sweet instruments hung up in cases that keep their
sounds to themselves. Why, I have often wished
myself poorer, that I might come nearer to you. We
are born to do benefits: and what better or
properer can we can our own than the riches of our
friends? O, what a precious comfort 'tis, to have
so many, like brothers, commanding one another's
fortunes! O joy, e'en made away ere 't can be born!
Mine eyes cannot hold out water, methinks: to
forget their faults, I drink to you.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Thou weepest to make them drink, Timon.

sentences.speaker: SECOND LORD
sentences.text:
Joy had the like conception in our eyes
And at that instant like a babe sprung up.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Ho, ho! I laugh to think that babe a bastard.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Ho, ho! I laugh to think that babe a bastard.

sentences.speaker: THIRD LORD
sentences.text: I promise you, my lord, you moved me much.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Much!
Tucket, within

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: What means that trump?
Enter a Servant

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: How now?

sentences.speaker: SERVANT
sentences.text:
Please you, my lord, there are certain
ladies most desirous of admittance.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Ladies! what are their wills?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Ladies! what are their wills?

sentences.speaker: SERVANT
sentences.text:
There comes with them a forerunner, my lord, which
bears that office, to signify their pleasures.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: I pray, let them be admitted.
Enter Cupid

sentences.speaker: CUPID
sentences.text:
Hail to thee, worthy Timon, and to all
That of his bounties taste! The five best senses
Acknowledge thee their patron; and come freely
To gratulate thy plenteous bosom: th' ear,
Taste, touch and smell, pleased from thy tale rise;
They only now come but to feast thine eyes.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
They're welcome all; let 'em have kind admittance:
Music, make their welcome!

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
They're welcome all; let 'em have kind admittance:
Music, make their welcome!
Exit Cupid

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD
sentences.text: You see, my lord, how ample you're beloved.
Music. Re-enter Cupid with a mask of Ladies as Amazons, with lutes in their hands, dancing and playing

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
Hoy-day, what a sweep of vanity comes this way!
They dance! they are mad women.
Like madness is the glory of this life.
As this pomp shows to a little oil and root.
We make ourselves fools, to disport ourselves;
And spend our flatteries, to drink those men
Upon whose age we void it up again,
With poisonous spite and envy.
Who lives that's not depraved or depraves?
Who dies, that bears not one spurn to their graves
Of their friends' gift?
I should fear those that dance before me now
Would one day stamp upon me: 't has been done;
Men shut their doors against a setting sun.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
TIMON
TIMON
FIRST LADY
sentences.text: My lord, you take us even at the best.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
'Faith, for the worst is filthy; and would not hold
taking, I doubt me.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Ladies, there is an idle banquet attends you:
Please you to dispose yourselves.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Ladies, there is an idle banquet attends you:
Please you to dispose yourselves.

sentences.speaker: ALL LADIES
sentences.text: Most thankfully, my lord.
Exeunt Cupid and Ladies

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Flavius.

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text: My lord?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: The little casket bring me hither.

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
Yes, my lord. More jewels yet!
There is no crossing him in 's humour;

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
Yes, my lord. More jewels yet!
There is no crossing him in 's humour;

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
Yes, my lord. More jewels yet!
There is no crossing him in 's humour;

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
Else I should tell him,—well, i' faith I should,
When all's spent, he 'ld be cross'd then, an he could.
'Tis pity bounty had not eyes behind,
That man might ne'er be wretched for his mind.

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
Else I should tell him,—well, i' faith I should,
When all's spent, he 'ld be cross'd then, an he could.
