sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text: What art thou there? speak.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text: What art thou there? speak.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
A beast, as thou art. The canker gnaw thy heart,
For showing me again the eyes of man!

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
A beast, as thou art. The canker gnaw thy heart,
For showing me again the eyes of man!

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text:
What is thy name? Is man so hateful to thee,
That art thyself a man?

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text:
What is thy name? Is man so hateful to thee,
That art thyself a man?

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text:
What is thy name? Is man so hateful to thee,
That art thyself a man?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
I am Misanthropos, and hate mankind.
For thy part, I do wish thou wert a dog,
That I might love thee something.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
I am Misanthropos, and hate mankind.
For thy part, I do wish thou wert a dog,
That I might love thee something.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
I am Misanthropos, and hate mankind.
For thy part, I do wish thou wert a dog,
That I might love thee something.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text:
I know thee well;
But in thy fortunes am unlearn'd and strange.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
I know thee too; and more than that I know thee,
I not desire to know. Follow thy drum;
With man's blood paint the ground, gules, gules:
Religious canons, civil laws are cruel;
Then what should war be? This fell whore of thine
Hath in her more destruction than thy sword,
For all her cherubim look.

sentences.speaker: PHRYNIA
sentences.text: Thy lips rot off!

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
I will not kiss thee; then the rot returns
To thine own lips again.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text: How came the noble Timon to this change?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
TIMON
TIMON
ALCIBIADES
sentences.text:
Noble Timon,
What friendship may I do thee?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
None, but to
Maintain my opinion.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text: What is it, Timon?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Promise me friendship, but perform none: if thou
wilt not promise, the gods plague thee, for thou art
a man! if thou dost perform, confound thee, for
thou art a man!

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:

wilt not promise, the gods plague thee, for thou art
a man! if thou dost perform, confound thee, for
thou art a man!

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text: I have heard in some sort of thy miseries.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Thou saw'st them, when I had prosperity.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text: I see them now; then was a blessed time.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text: I see them now; then was a blessed time.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: As thine is now, held with a brace of harlots.

sentences.speaker: TIMANDRA
sentences.text:
Is this the Athenian minion, whom the world
Voiced so regardfully?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Art thou Timandra?

sentences.speaker: TIMANDRA
sentences.text: Yes.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Be a whore still: they love thee not that use thee;
Give them diseases, leaving with thee their lust.
Make use of thy salt hours: season the slaves
For tubs and baths; bring down rose-cheeked youth
To the tub-fast and the diet.

sentences.speaker: TIMANDRA
sentences.text: Hang thee, monster!

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text:
Pardon him, sweet Timandra; for his wits
Are drown'd and lost in his calamities.
I have but little gold of late, brave Timon,
The want whereof doth daily make revolt
In my penurious band: I have heard, and grieved,
How cursed Athens, mindless of thy worth,
Forgetting thy great deeds, when neighbour states,
But for thy sword and fortune, trod upon them,—

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: I prithee, beat thy drum, and get thee gone.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text: I am thy friend, and pity thee, dear Timon.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text: I am thy friend, and pity thee, dear Timon.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
How dost thou pity him whom thou dost trouble?
I had rather be alone.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
How dost thou pity him whom thou dost trouble?
I had rather be alone.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text:
Why, fare thee well:
Here is some gold for thee.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text:
Why, fare thee well:
Here is some gold for thee.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Keep it, I cannot eat it.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Keep it, I cannot eat it.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text: When I have laid proud Athens on a heap,—

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Warr'st thou 'gainst Athens?

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text: Ay, Timon, and have cause.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
The gods confound them all in thy conquest;
And thee after, when thou hast conquer'd!

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text: Why me, Timon?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
That, by killing of villains,
Thou wast born to conquer my country.
Put up thy gold: go on,—here's gold,—go on;
Be as a planetary plague, when Jove
Will o'er some high-viced city hang his poison
In the sick air: let not thy sword skip one:
Pity not honour'd age for his white beard;
He is an usurer: strike me the counterfeit matron;
It is her habit only that is honest,
Herself's a bawd: let not the virgin's cheek
Make soft thy trenchant sword; for those milk-paps,
That through the window-bars bore at men's eyes,
Are not within the leaf of pity writ,
But set them down horrible traitors: spare not the
babe,
Whose dimpled smiles from fools exhaust their mercy;
Think it a bastard, whom the oracle
Hath doubtfully pronounced thy throat shall cut,
And mince it sans remorse: swear against objects;
Put armour on thine ears and on thine eyes;
Whose proof, nor yells of mothers, maids, nor babes,
Nor sight of priests in holy vestments bleeding,
Shall pierce a jot. There's gold to pay soldiers:
Make large confusion; and, thy fury spent,
Confounded be thyself! Speak not, be gone.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text:
Hast thou gold yet? I'll take the gold thou
givest me,
Not all thy counsel.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES

givest me,

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Dost thou, or dost thou not, heaven's curse
upon thee!

sentences.speaker: TIMANDRA
sentences.text: Give us some gold, good Timon: hast thou more?

sentences.speaker: TIMANDRA
sentences.text: Give us some gold, good Timon: hast thou more?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Enough to make a whore forswear her trade,
And to make whores, a bawd. Hold up, you sluts,
Your aprons mountant: you are not oathable,
Although, I know, you 'll swear, terribly swear
Into strong shudders and to heavenly agues
The immortal gods that hear you, —spare your oaths,
I'll trust to your conditions: be whores still;
And he whose pious breath seeks to convert you,
Be strong in whore, allure him, burn him up;
Let your close fire predominate his smoke,
And be no turncoats: yet may your pains, six months,
Be quite contrary: and thatch your poor thin roofs
With burthens of the dead;—some that were hang'd,
No matter:—wear them, betray with them: whore still;
Paint till a horse may mire upon your face,
A pox of wrinkles!

sentences.speaker: TIMANDRA
sentences.text:
Well, more gold: what then?
Believe't, that we'll do any thing for gold.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Consumptions sow
In hollow bones of man; strike their sharp shins,
And mar men's spurring. Crack the lawyer's voice,
That he may never more false title plead,
Nor sound his quillets shrilly: hoar the flamen,
That scolds against the quality of flesh,
And not believes himself: down with the nose,
Down with it flat; take the bridge quite away
Of him that, his particular to foresee,
Smells from the general weal: make curl'd-pate
ruffians bald;
And let the unscarr'd braggarts of the war
Derive some pain from you: plague all;
That your activity may defeat and quell
The source of all erection. There's more gold:
Do you damn others, and let this damn you,
And ditches grave you all!

More whore, more mischief first; I have given you
earnest.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text:
Strike up the drum towards Athens! Farewell, Timon:
If I thrive well, I'll visit thee again.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text:
Strike up the drum towards Athens! Farewell, Timon:
If I thrive well, I'll visit thee again.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text:
Strike up the drum towards Athens! Farewell, Timon:
If I thrive well, I'll visit thee again.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: If I hope well, I'll never see thee more.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text: I never did thee harm.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Yes, thou spokest well of me.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text: Call'st thou that harm?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Men daily find it. Get thee away, and take
Thy beagles with thee.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Men daily find it. Get thee away, and take
Thy beagles with thee.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text: We but offend him. Strike!

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
That nature, being sick of man's unkindness,
Should yet be hungry! Common mother, thou,

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
That nature, being sick of man's unkindness,
Should yet be hungry! Common mother, thou,

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Whose womb unmeasurable, and infinite breast,
Teems, and feeds all; whose self-same mettle,
Whereof thy proud child, arrogant man, is puff'd,
Engenders the black toad and adder blue,
The gilded newt and eyeless venom'd worm,
With all the abhorred births below crisp heaven
Whereon Hyperion's quickening fire doth shine;
Yield him, who all thy human sons doth hate,
From forth thy plenteous bosom, one poor root!
Ensear thy fertile and conceptious womb,
Let it no more bring out ingrateful man!
Go great with tigers, dragons, wolves, and bears;
Teem with new monsters, whom thy upward face
Hath to the marbled mansion all above
Never presented! —O, a root,—dear thanks!—
Dry up thy marrows, vines, and plough-torn leas;
Whereof ungrateful man, with liquorish draughts
And morsels unctuous, greases his pure mind,
That from it all consideration slips!

Enter APEMANTUS

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: More man? plague, plague!

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: More man? plague, plague!

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
I was directed hither: men report
Thou dost affect my manners, and dost use them.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
'Tis, then, because thou dost not keep a dog,
Whom I would imitate: consumption catch thee!

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
This is in thee a nature but infected;
A poor unmanly melancholy sprung
From change of fortune. Why this spade? this place?
This slave-like habit? and these looks of care?
Thy flatterers yet wear silk, drink wine, lie soft;
Hug their diseased perfumes, and have forgot
That ever Timon was. Shame not these woods,
By putting on the cunning of a carper.
Be thou a flatterer now, and seek to thrive
By that which has undone thee: hinge thy knee,
And let his very breath, whom thou'lt observe,
Blow off thy cap; praise his most vicious strain,
And call it excellent: thou wast told thus;
Thou gavest thine ears like tapsters that bid welcome
To knaves and all approachers: 'tis most just
That thou turn rascal; hadst thou wealth again,
Rascals should have 't. Do not assume my likeness.

And call it excellent: thou wast told thus;
Thou gavest thine ears like tapsters that bid welcome
To knaves and all approachers: 'tis most just
That thou turn rascal; hadst thou wealth again,
Rascals should have 't. Do not assume my likeness.

This is in thee a nature but infected;
A poor unmanly melancholy sprung
From change of fortune. Why this spade? this place?
This slave-like habit? and these looks of care?
Thy flatterers yet wear silk, drink wine, lie soft;
Hug their diseased perfumes, and have forgot
That ever Timon was. Shame not these woods,
By putting on the cunning of a carper.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Were I like thee, I'ld throw away myself.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
Thou hast cast away thyself, being like thyself;
A madman so long, now a fool. What, think'st
That the bleak air, thy boisterous chamberlain,
Will put thy shirt on warm? will these moss'd trees,
That have outlived the eagle, page thy heels,
And skip where thou point'st out? will the
cold brook,
Candied with ice, caudle thy morning taste,
To cure thy o'er-night's surfeit? Call the creatures
Whose naked natures live in an the spite
Of wreakful heaven, whose bare unhoused trunks,
To the conflicting elements exposed,
Answer mere nature; bid them flatter thee;
O, thou shalt find—

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: A fool of thee: depart.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: A fool of thee: depart.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: I love thee better now than e'er I did.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: I hate thee worse.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Why?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Thou flatter'st misery.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: I flatter not; but say thou art a caitiff.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: I flatter not; but say thou art a caitiff.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Why dost thou seek me out?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: To vex thee.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Always a villain's office or a fool's.
Dost please thyself in't?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Always a villain's office or a fool's.
Dost please thyself in't?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Ay.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: What! a knave too?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: What! a knave too?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
If thou didst put this sour-cold habit on
To castigate thy pride, 'twere well: but thou
Dost it enforcedly; thou'ldst courtier be again,
Wert thou not beggar. Willing misery
Outlives encertain pomp, is crown'd before:
The one is filling still, never complete;
The other, at high wish: best state, contentless,
Hath a distracted and most wretched being,
Worse than the worst, content.
Thou shouldst desire to die, being miserable.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Not by his breath that is more miserable.
Thou art a slave, whom Fortune's tender arm
With favour never clasp'd; but bred a dog.
Hadst thou, like us from our first swath, proceeded
The sweet degrees that this brief world affords
To such as may the passive drugs of it
Freely command, thou wouldst have plunged thyself
In general riot; melted down thy youth
In different beds of lust; and never learn'd
The icy precepts of respect, but follow'd
The sugar'd game before thee. But myself,
Who had the world as my confectionary,
The mouths, the tongues, the eyes and hearts of men
At duty, more than I could frame employment,
That numberless upon me stuck as leaves
Do on the oak, hive with one winter's brush
Fell from their boughs and left me open, bare
For every storm that blows: I, to bear this,
That never knew but better, is some burden:
Thy nature did commence in sufferance, time
Hath made thee hard in't. Why shouldst thou hate men?
They never flatter'd thee: what hast thou given?
If thou wilt curse, thy father, that poor rag,
Must be thy subject, who in spite put stuff
To some she beggar and compounded thee
Poor rogue hereditary. Hence, be gone!
If thou hadst not been born the worst of men,
Thou hadst been a knave and flatterer.

Poor rogue hereditary. Hence, be gone!
If thou hadst not been born the worst of men,
Thou hadst been a knave and flatterer.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Not by his breath that is more miserable.
Thou art a slave, whom Fortune's tender arm
With favour never clasp'd; but bred a dog.
Hadst thou, like us from our first swath, proceeded
The sweet degrees that this brief world affords
To such as may the passive drugs of it
Freely command, thou wouldst have plunged thyself
In general riot; melted down thy youth
In different beds of lust; and never learn'd
The icy precepts of respect, but follow'd
The sugar'd game before thee. But myself,
Who had the world as my confectionary,
The mouths, the tongues, the eyes and hearts of men
At duty, more than I could frame employment,
That numberless upon me stuck as leaves
Do on the oak, hive with one winter's brush
Fell from their boughs and left me open, bare
For every storm that blows: I, to bear this,
That never knew but better, is some burden:
Thy nature did commence in sufferance, time
Hath made thee hard in't. Why shouldst thou hate men?
They never flatter'd thee: what hast thou given?
If thou wilt curse, thy father, that poor rag,
Must be thy subject, who in spite put stuff
To some she beggar and compounded thee
Poor rogue hereditary. Hence, be gone!
If thou hadst not been born the worst of men,
Thou hadst been a knave and flatterer.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Art thou proud yet?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Art thou proud yet?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Ay, that I am not thee.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
I, that I was
No prodigal.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
I, that I am one now:
Were all the wealth I have shut up in thee,
I'ld give thee leave to hang it. Get thee gone.
That the whole life of Athens were in this!
Thus would I eat it.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Here; I will mend thy feast.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Here; I will mend thy feast.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: First mend my company, take away thyself.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: So I shall mend mine own, by the lack of thine.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
'Tis not well mended so, it is but botch'd;
if not, I would it were.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: What wouldst thou have to Athens?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Thee thither in a whirlwind. If thou wilt,
Tell them there I have gold; look, so I have.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Thee thither in a whirlwind. If thou wilt,
Tell them there I have gold; look, so I have.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Here is no use for gold.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
The best and truest;
For here it sleeps, and does no hired harm.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Where liest o' nights, Timon?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Under that's above me.
Where feed'st thou o' days, Apemantus?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Under that's above me.
Where feed'st thou o' days, Apemantus?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
Where my stomach finds meat; or, rather, where I eat
it.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Would poison were obedient and knew my mind!

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Where wouldst thou send it?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: To sauce thy dishes.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
The middle of humanity thou never knewest, but the
extremity of both ends: when thou wast in thy gilt
and thy perfume, they mocked thee for too much
curiosity; in thy rags thou knowest none, but art
despised for the contrary. There's a medlar for
thee, eat it.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: On what I hate I feed not.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Dost hate a medlar?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Ay, though it look like thee.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Ay, though it look like thee.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
An thou hadst hated meddlers sooner, thou shouldst
have loved thyself better now. What man didst thou
ever know unthrift that was beloved after his means?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
An thou hadst hated meddlers sooner, thou shouldst
have loved thyself better now. What man didst thou
ever know unthrift that was beloved after his means?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Who, without those means thou talkest of, didst thou
ever know beloved?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Myself.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
I understand thee; thou hadst some means to keep a
dog.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
I understand thee; thou hadst some means to keep a
dog.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
What things in the world canst thou nearest compare
to thy flatterers?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Women nearest; but men, men are the things
themselves. What wouldst thou do with the world,
Apemantus, if it lay in thy power?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Women nearest; but men, men are the things
themselves. What wouldst thou do with the world,
Apemantus, if it lay in thy power?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Women nearest; but men, men are the things
themselves. What wouldst thou do with the world,
Apemantus, if it lay in thy power?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Give it the beasts, to be rid of the men.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Wouldst thou have thyself fall in the confusion of
men, and remain a beast with the beasts?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Ay, Timon.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
A beastly ambition, which the gods grant thee t'
attain to! If thou wert the lion, the fox would
beguile thee; if thou wert the lamb, the fox would
eat three: if thou wert the fox, the lion would
suspect thee, when peradventure thou wert accused by
the ass: if thou wert the ass, thy dulness would
torment thee, and still thou livedst but as a
breakfast to the wolf: if thou wert the wolf, thy
greediness would afflict thee, and oft thou shouldst
hazard thy life for thy dinner: wert thou the
unicorn, pride and wrath would confound thee and
make thine own self the conquest of thy fury: wert
thou a bear, thou wouldst be killed by the horse:
wert thou a horse, thou wouldst be seized by the
leopard: wert thou a leopard, thou wert german to
the lion and the spots of thy kindred were jurors on
thy life: all thy safety were remotion and thy
defence absence. What beast couldst thou be, that
were not subject to a beast? and what a beast art
thou already, that seest not thy loss in
transformation!

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
If thou couldst please me with speaking to me, thou
mightst have hit upon it here: the commonwealth of
Athens is become a forest of beasts.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
How has the ass broke the wall, that thou art out of
the city?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
How has the ass broke the wall, that thou art out of
the city?

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text:
Yonder comes a poet and a painter: the plague of
company light upon thee! I will fear to catch it
and give way: when I know not what else to do, I'll
see thee again.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
When there is nothing living but thee, thou shalt be
welcome. I had rather be a beggar's dog than Apemantus.

sentences.speaker: TIMON

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Thou art the cap of all the fools alive.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Would thou wert clean enough to spit upon!

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: A plague on thee! thou art too bad to curse.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: A plague on thee! thou art too bad to curse.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: All villains that do stand by thee are pure.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: There is no leprosy but what thou speak'st.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
If I name thee.
I'll beat thee, but I should infect my hands.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS

sentences.speaker: TIMON
TIMON
TIMON
APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Would thou wouldst burst!

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Away,
Thou tedious rogue! I am sorry I shall lose
A stone by thee.

sentences.speaker: TIMON

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Beast!

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Slave!

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Toad!

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Rogue, rogue, rogue!
I am sick of this false world, and will love nought
But even the mere necessities upon 't.
Then, Timon, presently prepare thy grave;
Lie where the light foam the sea may beat
Thy grave-stone daily: make thine epitaph,
That death in me at others' lives may laugh.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
O thou sweet king-killer, and dear divorce
'Twixt natural son and sire! thou bright defiler
Of Hymen's purest bed! thou valiant Mars!
Thou ever young, fresh, loved and delicate wooer,
Whose blush doth thaw the consecrated snow
That lies on Dian's lap! thou visible god,
That solder'st close impossibilities,
And makest them kiss! that speak'st with
every tongue,
To every purpose! O thou touch of hearts!
Think, thy slave man rebels, and by thy virtue
Set them into confounding odds, that beasts
May have the world in empire!

Would 'twere so!
But not till I am dead. I'll say thou'st gold:
Thou wilt be throng'd to shortly.

sentences.speaker: TIMON

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Ay.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Thy back, I prithee.

sentences.speaker: APEMANTUS
sentences.text: Live, and love thy misery.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Long live so, and so die.
Exit APEMANTUS

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
I am quit.
Moe things like men! Eat, Timon, and abhor them.

Enter Banditti

sentences.speaker: FIRST BANDIT
sentences.text:
Where should he have this gold? It is some poor
fragment, some slender sort of his remainder: the
mere want of gold, and the falling-from of his
friends, drove him into this melancholy.

sentences.speaker: SECOND BANDIT

sentences.speaker: THIRD BANDIT
sentences.text:
Let us make the assay upon him: if he care not
for't, he will supply us easily; if he covetously
reserve it, how shall's get it?

sentences.speaker: FIRST BANDIT
sentences.text: Is not this he?

sentences.speaker: BANDITTI
sentences.text: Where?

sentences.speaker: SECOND BANDIT
sentences.text: 'Tis his description.

sentences.speaker: THIRD BANDIT
sentences.text: He; I know him.

sentences.speaker: THIRD BANDIT
sentences.text: He; I know him.

sentences.speaker: BANDITTI
sentences.text: Save thee, Timon.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Now, thieves?

sentences.speaker: BANDITTI
sentences.text: Soldiers, not thieves.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Both too; and women's sons.

sentences.speaker: BANDITTI
sentences.text: We are not thieves, but men that much do want.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
TIMON
TIMON
TIMON
TIMON
TIMON
FIRST BANDIT
sentences.text:
We cannot live on grass, on berries, water,
As beasts and birds and fishes.

sentences.speaker: FIRST BANDIT
sentences.text:
We cannot live on grass, on berries, water,
As beasts and birds and fishes.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Nor on the beasts themselves, the birds, and fishes;
You must eat men. Yet thanks I must you con
That you are thieves profess'd, that you work not
In holier shapes: for there is boundless theft
In limited professions. Rascal thieves,
Here's gold. Go, suck the subtle blood o' the grape,
Till the high fever seethe your blood to froth,
And so 'scape hanging: trust not the physician;
His antidotes are poison, and he slays
Moe than you rob: take wealth and lives together;
Do villany, do, since you protest to do't,
Like workmen. I'll example you with thievery.
The sun's a thief, and with his great attraction
Robs the vast sea: the moon's an arrant thief,
And her pale fire she snatches from the sun:
The sea's a thief, whose liquid surge resolves
The moon into salt tears: the earth's a thief,
That feeds and breeds by a composture stolen
From general excrement : each thing's a thief:
The laws, your curb and whip, in their rough power
Have uncheque'd theft. Love not yourselves: away,
Rob one another. There's more gold. Cut throats:
All that you meet are thieves: to Athens go,
Break open shops; nothing can you steal,
But thieves do lose it: steal no less for this
I give you; and gold confound you howsoe'er! Amen.

sentences.speaker: THIRD BANDIT
sentences.text:
Has almost charmed me from my profession, by
persuading me to it.

sentences.speaker: FIRST BANDIT
sentences.text:
'Tis in the malice of mankind that he thus advises
us; not to have us thrive in our mystery.

sentences.speaker: SECOND BANDIT
sentences.text: I'll believe him as an enemy, and give over my trade.

sentences.speaker: FIRST BANDIT
sentences.text:
Let us first see peace in Athens: there is no time
so miserable but a man may be true.

sentences.speaker: FIRST BANDIT
sentences.text:
Let us first see peace in Athens: there is no time
so miserable but a man may be true.
Exeunt Banditti
Enter FLAVIUS

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
O you gods!
Is yond despised and ruinous man my lord?
Full of decay and failing? O monument
And wonder of good deeds evilly bestow'd!
What an alteration of honour
Has desperate want made!
What viler thing upon the earth than friends
Who can bring noblest minds to basest ends!
How rarely does it meet with this time's guise,
When man was wish'd to love his enemies!
Grant I may ever love, and rather woo
Those that would mischief me than those that do!
Has caught me in his eye: I will present
My honest grief unto him; and, as my lord,
Still serve him with my life. My dearest master!

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
O you gods!
Is yond despised and ruinous man my lord?
Full of decay and failing? O monument
And wonder of good deeds evilly bestow'd!
What an alteration of honour
Has desperate want made!
What viler thing upon the earth than friends
Who can bring noblest minds to basest ends!
How rarely does it meet with this time's guise,
When man was wish'd to love his enemies!
Grant I may ever love, and rather woo
Those that would mischief me than those that do!
Has caught me in his eye: I will present
My honest grief unto him; and, as my lord,
Still serve him with my life. My dearest master!

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Away! what art thou?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Away! what art thou?

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text: Have you forgot me, sir?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
TIMON
TIMON
FLAVIUS
sentences.text: An honest poor servant of yours.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Then I know thee not:
I never had honest man about me, I; all
I kept were knaves, to serve in meat to villains.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Then I know thee not:
I never had honest man about me, I; all
I kept were knaves, to serve in meat to villains.

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
The gods are witness,
Ne'er did poor steward wear a truer grief
For his undone lord than mine eyes for you.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
What, dost thou weep? Come nearer. Then I
love thee,
Because thou art a woman, and disclaim'st
Flinty mankind; whose eyes do never give
But thorough lust and laughter. Pity's sleeping:
Strange times, that weep with laughing, not with
weeping!

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
I beg of you to know me, good my lord,
To accept my grief and whilst this poor wealth lasts
To entertain me as your steward still.

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
I beg of you to know me, good my lord,
To accept my grief and whilst this poor wealth lasts
To entertain me as your steward still.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Had I a steward
So true, so just, and now so comfortable?
It almost turns my dangerous nature mild.
Let me behold thy face. Surely, this man
Was born of woman.
Forgive my general and exceptless rashness,
You perpetual-sober gods! I do proclaim
One honest man—mistake me not—but one;
No more, I pray,—and he's a steward.
How fain would I have hated all mankind!
And thou redeem'st thyself: but all, save thee,
I fell with curses.
Methinks thou art more honest now than wise;
For, by oppressing and betraying me,
Thou mightst have sooner got another service:
For many so arrive at second masters,
Upon their first lord's neck. But tell me true—
For I must ever doubt, though ne'er so sure—
Is not thy kindness subtle, covetous,
If not a usuring kindness, and, as rich men deal gifts,
Expecting in return twenty for one?

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
No, my most worthy master; in whose breast
Doubt and suspect, alas, are placed too late:
You should have fear'd false times when you did feast:
Suspect still comes where an estate is least.
That which I show, heaven knows, is merely love,
Duty and zeal to your unmatched mind,
Care of your food and living; and, believe it,
My most honour'd lord,
For any benefit that points to me,
Either in hope or present, I'ld exchange
For this one wish, that you had power and wealth
To requite me, by making rich yourself.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Look thee, 'tis so! Thou singly honest man,
Here, take: the gods out of my misery
Have sent thee treasure. Go, live rich and happy;
But thus condition'd: thou shalt build from men;
Hate all, curse all, show charity to none,
But let the famish'd flesh slide from the bone,
Ere thou relieve the beggar; give to dogs
What thou deny'st to men; let prisons swallow 'em,
Debts wither 'em to nothing; be men like
blasted woods,
And may diseases lick up their false bloods!
And so farewell and thrive.

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
O, let me stay,
And comfort you, my master.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
If thou hatest curses,
Stay not; fly, whilst thou art blest and free:
Ne'er see thou man, and let me ne'er see thee.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
If thou hatest curses,
Stay not; fly, whilst thou art blest and free:
Ne'er see thou man, and let me ne'er see thee.
Exit FLAVIUS. TIMON retires to his cave
Enter POET and PAINTER; TIMON watching them from his cave

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text:
As I took note of the place, it cannot be far where
he abides.

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text:
What's to be thought of him? does the rumour hold
for true, that he's so full of gold?

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text:
What's to be thought of him? does the rumour hold
for true, that he's so full of gold?

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text:
Certain: Alcibiades reports it; Phrynia and
Timandra had gold of him: he likewise enriched poor
straggling soldiers with great quantity: 'tis said
he gave unto his steward a mighty sum.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text:
Certain: Alcibiades reports it; Phrynia and
Timandra had gold of him: he likewise enriched poor
straggling soldiers with great quantity: 'tis said
he gave unto his steward a mighty sum.

he gave unto his steward a mighty sum.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text:
Certain: Alcibiades reports it; Phrynia and
Timandra had gold of him: he likewise enriched poor
straggling soldiers with great quantity: 'tis said
he gave unto his steward a mighty sum.

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text:
Then this breaking of his has been but a try for his
friends.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text:
Nothing else: you shall see him a palm in Athens
again, and flourish with the highest. Therefore
'tis not amiss we tender our loves to him, in this
supposed distress of his: it will show honestly in
us; and is very likely to load our purposes with
what they travail for, if it be a just true report
that goes of his having.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text: What have you now to present unto him?

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text:
Nothing at this time but my visitation: only I will
promise him an excellent piece.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text:
Nothing at this time but my visitation: only I will
promise him an excellent piece.

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text:
I must serve him so too, tell him of an intent
that's coming toward him.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text:
Good as the best. Promising is the very air o' the
time: it opens the eyes of expectation:
performance is ever the duller for his act; and,
but in the plainer and simpler kind of people, the
deed of saying is quite out of use. To promise is
most courtly and fashionable: performance is a kind
of will or testament which argues a great sickness
in his judgment that makes it.

but in the plainer and simpler kind of people, the
deed of saying is quite out of use. To promise is
most courtly and fashionable: performance is a kind
of will or testament which argues a great sickness
in his judgment that makes it.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
[Aside] Excellent workman! thou canst not paint a
man so bad as is thyself.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
[Aside] Excellent workman! thou canst not paint a
man so bad as is thyself.

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text:
I am thinking what I shall say I have provided for
him: it must be a personating of himself; a satire
against the softness of prosperity, with a discovery
of the infinite flatteries that follow youth and
opulency.

sentences.text:
[Aside] Must thou needs stand for a villain in
thine own work? wilt thou whip thine own faults in
other men? Do so, I have gold for thee.

Nay, let's seek him:
Then do we sin against our own estate,
When we may profit meet, and come too late.

TIMON
sentences.text:
[Aside] I'll meet you at the turn. What a
god's gold,
That he is worshipp'd in a baser temple
Than where swine feed!
'Tis thou that rigg'st the bark and plough'st the foam,
Settlest admired reverence in a slave:
To thee be worship! and thy saints for aye
Be crown'd with plagues that thee alone obey!
Fit I meet them.

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text: Hail, worthy Timon!

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text: Our late noble master!

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Have I once lived to see two honest men?

sentences.speaker: POET
sentences.text:
Sir,
Having often of your open bounty tasted,
Hearing you were retired, your friends fall'n off,
Whose thankless natures—O abhorred spirits!—
Not all the whips of heaven are large enough:
What! to you,
Whose star-like nobleness gave life and influence
To their whole being! I am rapt and cannot cover
The monstrous bulk of this ingratitude
With any size of words.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Let it go naked, men may see't the better:
You that are honest, by being what you are,
Make them best seen and known.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text:
He and myself
Have travail'd in the great shower of your gifts,
And sweetly felt it.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Ay, you are honest men.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER
sentences.text: We are hither come to offer you our service.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Most honest men! Why, how shall I requite you?
Can you eat roots, and drink cold water? no.

sentences.speaker: TIMON

sentences.speaker: BOTH
sentences.text: What we can do, we'll do, to do you service.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Ye're honest men: ye've heard that I have gold;
I am sure you have: speak truth; ye're honest men.

sentences.speaker: TIMON

sentences.speaker: PAINTER

So it is said, my noble lord; but therefore
Came not my friend nor I.

sentences.speaker: PAINTER

sentences.speaker: TIMON

PAINTER
sentences.text: So, so, my lord.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
E'en so, sir, as I say. And, for thy fiction,
Why, thy verse swells with stuff so fine and smooth
That thou art even natural in thine art.
But, for all this, my honest-natured friends,
I must needs say you have a little fault:
Marry, 'tis not monstrous in you, neither wish I
You take much pains to mend.

Beseech your honour
To make it known to us.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: You'll take it ill.

sentences.speaker: BOTH
sentences.text: Most thankfully, my lord.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Will you, indeed?

sentences.speaker: BOTH
sentences.text: Doubt it not, worthy lord.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
There's never a one of you but trusts a knave,
That mightily deceives you.

sentences.speaker: BOTH
sentences.text: Do we, my lord?

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Ay, and you hear him cog, see him dissemble,
Know his gross patchery, love him, feed him,
Keep in your bosom: yet remain assured
That he's a made-up villain.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: PAINTER

sentences.speaker: POET

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Look you, I love you well; I'll give you gold,
Rid me these villains from your companies:
Hang them or stab them, drown them in a draught,
Confound them by some course, and come to me,
I'll give you gold enough.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
You that way and you this, but two in company;
Each man apart, all single and alone,
Yet an arch-villain keeps him company.
If where thou art two villains shall not be,
Come not near him. If thou wouldst not reside
But where one villain is, then him abandon.
Hence, pack! there's gold; you came for gold, ye
slaves:

You are an alchemist; make gold of that.

Enter FLAVIUS and two Senators

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
It is in vain that you would speak with Timon;
For he is set so only to himself
That nothing but himself which looks like man
Is friendly with him.

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
It is in vain that you would speak with Timon;
For he is set so only to himself
That nothing but himself which looks like man
Is friendly with him.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:
Bring us to his cave:
It is our part and promise to the Athenians
To speak with Timon.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:
Bring us to his cave:
It is our part and promise to the Athenians
To speak with Timon.

sentences.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
sentences.text:
At all times alike
Men are not still the same: 'twas time and griefs
That framed him thus: time, with his fairer hand,
Offering the fortunes of his former days,
The former man may make him. Bring us to him,
And chance it as it may.

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
Here is his cave.
Peace and content be here! Lord Timon! Timon!
Look out, and speak to friends: the Athenians,
By two of their most reverend senate, greet thee:
Speak to them, noble Timon.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Thou sun, that comfort'st, burn! Speak, and
be hang'd:
For each true word, a blister! and each false
Be as cauterizing to the root o' the tongue,
Consuming it with speaking!

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text: Worthy Timon,—

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Of none but such as you, and you of Timon.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text: The senators of Athens greet thee, Timon.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
I thank them; and would send them back the plague,
Could I but catch it for them.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:
O, forget
What we are sorry for ourselves in thee.
The senators with one consent of love
Entreat thee back to Athens; who have thought
On special dignities, which vacant lie
For thy best use and wearing.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:

On special dignities, which vacant lie
For thy best use and wearing.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:
O, forget
What we are sorry for ourselves in thee.
The senators with one consent of love
Entreat thee back to Athens; who have thought
On special dignities, which vacant lie
For thy best use and wearing.

sentences.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
sentences.text:
They confess
Toward thee forgetfulness too general, gross:
Which now the public body, which doth seldom
Play the recanter, feeling in itself
A lack of Timon's aid, hath sense withal
Of its own fail, restraining aid to Timon;
And send forth us, to make their sorrow'd render,
Together with a recompense more fruitful
Than their offence can weigh down by the dram;
Ay, even such heaps and sums of love and wealth
As shall to thee blot out what wrongs were theirs
And write in thee the figures of their love,
Ever to read them thine.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
You witch me in it;
Surprise me to the very brink of tears:
Lend me a fool's heart and a woman's eyes,
And I'll beweep these comforts, worthy senators.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
You witch me in it;

And I'll beweep these comforts, worthy senators.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:
Therefore, so please thee to return with us
And of our Athens, thine and ours, to take
The captainship, thou shalt be met with thanks,
Allow'd with absolute power and thy good name
Live with authority: so soon we shall drive back
Of Alcibiades the approaches wild,
Who, like a boar too savage, doth root up
His country's peace.

And shakes his threatening sword
Against the walls of Athens.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text: Therefore, Timon,—

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Well, sir, I will; therefore, I will, sir; thus:
If Alcibiades kill my countrymen,
Let Alcibiades know this of Timon,
That Timon cares not. But if be sack fair Athens,
And take our goodly aged men by the beards,
Giving our holy virgins to the stain
Of contumelious, beastly, mad-brain'd war,
Then let him know, and tell him Timon speaks it,
In pity of our aged and our youth,
I cannot choose but tell him, that I care not,
And let him take't at worst; for their knives care not,
While you have throats to answer: for myself,
There's not a whittle in the unruly camp
But I do prize it at my love before
The reverend'st throat in Athens. So I leave you
To the protection of the prosperous gods,
As thieves to keepers.

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text: Stay not, all's in vain.

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text: Stay not, all's in vain.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
TIMON
TIMON
TIMON
TIMON
FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text: We speak in vain.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
But yet I love my country, and am not
One that rejoices in the common wreck,
As common bruit doth put it.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text: That's well spoke.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text: Commend me to my loving countrymen,—

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:
These words become your lips as they pass
thorough them.

And enter in our ears like great triumphers
In their applauding gates.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Commend me to them,
And tell them that, to ease them of their griefs,
Their fears of hostile strokes, their aches, losses,
Their pangs of love, with other incident throes
That nature's fragile vessel doth sustain
In life's uncertain voyage, I will some kindness do
them:
I'll teach them to prevent wild Alcibiades' wrath.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text: I like this well; he will return again.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text: I like this well; he will return again.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
I have a tree, which grows here in my close,
That mine own use invites me to cut down,
And shortly must I fell it: tell my friends,
Tell Athens, in the sequence of degree
From high to low throughout, that whoso please
To stop affliction, let him take his haste,
Come hither, ere my tree hath felt the axe,
And hang himself. I pray you, do my greeting.

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text: Trouble him no further; thus you still shall find him.

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text: Trouble him no further; thus you still shall find him.

sentences.speaker: TIMON
sentences.text:
Come not to me again: but say to Athens,
Timon hath made his everlasting mansion
Upon the beached verge of the salt flood;
Who once a day with his embossed froth
The turbulent surge shall cover: thither come,
And let my grave-stone be your oracle.
Lips, let sour words go by and language end:
What is amiss plague and infection mend!
Graves only be men's works and death their gain!
Sun, hide thy beams! Timon hath done his reign.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:
His discontents are unremoveably
Coupled to nature.

sentences.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
sentences.text:
Our hope in him is dead: let us return,
And strain what other means is left unto us
In our dear peril.

sentences.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
sentences.text:
Our hope in him is dead: let us return,
And strain what other means is left unto us
In our dear peril.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text: It requires swift foot.
Exeunt
Enter two Senators and a Messenger

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:
Thou hast painfully discover'd: are his files
As full as thy report?

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:
Thou hast painfully discover'd: are his files
As full as thy report?

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
I have spoke the least:
Besides, his expedition promises
Present approach.

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
I have spoke the least:
Besides, his expedition promises
Present approach.

sentences.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
sentences.text: We stand much hazard, if they bring not Timon.

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
I met a courier, one mine ancient friend;
Whom, though in general part we were opposed,
Yet our old love made a particular force,
And made us speak like friends: this man was riding
From Alcibiades to Timon's cave,
With letters of entreaty, which imported
His fellowship i' the cause against your city,
In part for his sake moved.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text: Here come our brothers.
Enter the Senators from TIMON

sentences.speaker: THIRD SENATOR
sentences.text:
No talk of Timon, nothing of him expect.
The enemies' drum is heard, and fearful scouring
Doth choke the air with dust: in, and prepare:
Ours is the fall, I fear; our foes the snare.

Enter a Soldier, seeking TIMON

sentences.speaker: SOLDIER
sentences.text:
By all description this should be the place.
Who's here? speak, ho! No answer! What is this?
Timon is dead, who hath outstretch'd his span:
Some beast rear'd this; there does not live a man.
Dead, sure; and this his grave. What's on this tomb
I cannot read; the character I'll take with wax:
Our captain hath in every figure skill,
An aged interpreter, though young in days:
Before proud Athens he's set down by this,
Whose fall the mark of his ambition is.

Trumpets sound. Enter ALCIBIADES with his powers

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text:
Sound to this coward and lascivious town
Our terrible approach.
A parley sounded
Enter Senators on the walls

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text:
Till now you have gone on and fill'd the time
With all licentious measure, making your wills
The scope of justice; till now myself and such
As slept within the shadow of your power
Have wander'd with our traversed arms and breathed
Our sufferance vainly: now the time is flush,
When crouching marrow in the bearer strong
Cries of itself 'No more:' now breathless wrong
Shall sit and pant in your great chairs of ease,
And pursy insolence shall break his wind
With fear and horrid flight.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:
Noble and young,
When thy first griefs were but a mere conceit,
Ere thou hadst power or we had cause of fear,
We sent to thee, to give thy rages balm,
To wipe out our ingratitude with loves
Above their quantity.

sentences.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
sentences.text:
So did we woo
Transformed Timon to our city's love
By humble message and by promised means:
We were not all unkind, nor all deserve
The common stroke of war.

sentences.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
sentences.text:
So did we woo
Transformed Timon to our city's love
By humble message and by promised means:
We were not all unkind, nor all deserve
The common stroke of war.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:
These walls of ours
Were not erected by their hands from whom
You have received your griefs; nor are they such
That these great towers, trophies and schools
should fall
For private faults in them.

sentences.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
sentences.text:
Nor are they living
Who were the motives that you first went out;
Shame that they wanted cunning, in excess
Hath broke their hearts. March, noble lord,
Into our city with thy banners spread:
By decimation, and a tithed death—
If thy revenges hunger for that food
Which nature loathes—take thou the destined tenth,
And by the hazard of the spotted die
Let die the spotted.

Let die the spotted.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:
All have not offended;
For those that were, it is not square to take
On those that are, revenges: crimes, like lands,
Are not inherited. Then, dear countryman,
Bring in thy ranks, but leave without thy rage:
Spare thy Athenian cradle and those kin
Which in the bluster of thy wrath must fall
With those that have offended: like a shepherd,
Approach the fold and cull the infected forth,
But kill not all together.

On those that are, revenges: crimes, like lands,
Are not inherited. Then, dear countryman,
Bring in thy ranks, but leave without thy rage:
Spare thy Athenian cradle and those kin
Which in the bluster of thy wrath must fall
With those that have offended: like a shepherd,
Approach the fold and cull the infected forth,
But kill not all together.

sentences.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
sentences.text:
What thou wilt,
Thou rather shalt enforce it with thy smile
Than hew to't with thy sword.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:
Set but thy foot
Against our rampired gates, and they shall ope;
So thou wilt send thy gentle heart before,
To say thou'lt enter friendly.

sentences.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
sentences.text:
Throw thy glove,
Or any token of thine honour else,
That thou wilt use the wars as thy redress
And not as our confusion, all thy powers
Shall make their harbour in our town, till we
Have seal'd thy full desire.

sentences.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
sentences.text:
Throw thy glove,
Or any token of thine honour else,
That thou wilt use the wars as thy redress
And not as our confusion, all thy powers
Shall make their harbour in our town, till we
Have seal'd thy full desire.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text:
Then there's my glove;
Descend, and open your uncharged ports:
Those enemies of Timon's and mine own
Whom you yourselves shall set out for reproof
Fall and no more: and, to atone your fears
With my more noble meaning, not a man
Shall pass his quarter, or offend the stream
Of regular justice in your city's bounds,
But shall be render'd to your public laws
At heaviest answer.

sentences.speaker: BOTH
sentences.text: 'Tis most nobly spoken.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text: Descend, and keep your words.

sentences.speaker: ALCIBIADES
sentences.text: Descend, and keep your words.
Enter Soldier

sentences.speaker: SOLDIER
SOLDIER
ALCIBIADES
sentences.text:
[Reads the epitaph] 'Here lies a
wretched corse, of wretched soul bereft:
Seek not my name: a plague consume you wicked
caitiffs left!
Here lie I, Timon; who, alive, all living men did hate:
Pass by and curse thy fill, but pass and stay
not here thy gait.'
These well express in thee thy latter spirits:
Though thou abhorr'dst in us our human griefs,
Scorn'dst our brain's flow and those our
droplets which
From niggard nature fall, yet rich conceit
Taught thee to make vast Neptune weep for aye
On thy low grave, on faults forgiven. Dead
Is noble Timon: of whose memory
Hereafter more. Bring me into your city,
And I will use the olive with my sword,
Make war breed peace, make peace stint war, make each
Prescribe to other as each other's leech.
Let our drums strike.
